title.zh: 卷六十五 東方朔傳
title.raw: 卷六十五 東方朔傳 Volume 65: Dongfang Shuo

sentences.zh: 卷六十五 東方朔傳第三十五

translations.literal: Volume 65: Biography of Dongfang Shuo, Number 35.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 65: the thirty-fifth biography—Dongfang Shuo.

sentences.zh: 東方朔字曼倩，平原厭次人也。

translations.literal: Dongfang Shuo, courtesy name Manqian, was a man of Yanci in Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo, whose courtesy name was Manqian, came from Yanci in Pingyuan commandery.

sentences.zh: 武帝初即位，征天下舉方正賢良文學材力之士，待以不次之位，四方士多上書言得失，自衒鬻者以千數，其不足采者輒報聞罷。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu had first ascended the throne, he summoned from the realm those recommended for square integrity, worthy excellence, literary learning, and strength of talent, treating them with appointments outside the usual sequence; knights from the four quarters largely submitted memorials discussing gain and loss; those who displayed and hawked themselves numbered in the thousands; those not worth gathering were always answered with heard and dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Wu's reign the court summoned men of integrity, literary talent, and practical ability, promising posts outside the normal ladder of promotion. Memorials poured in from every quarter—thousands of men peddling their wares—and those without merit received a polite note of receipt and heard no more.

sentences.zh: 朔初來，上書曰：「臣朔少失父母，長養兄嫂。

translations.literal: When Shuo first arrived, he submitted a memorial saying: Your servant Shuo in youth lost father and mother and was brought up by his elder brother and sister-in-law.
translations.idiomatic: In his first memorial Dongfang Shuo wrote, I lost my parents while young and was raised by my brother and his wife.

sentences.zh: 年十三學書，三冬文史足用。

translations.literal: At age thirteen he studied writing; in three winters his command of documents and histories sufficed for use.
translations.idiomatic: By thirteen he had mastered literacy; three winters of study gave him all the documentary learning he needed.

sentences.zh: 十五學擊劍。

translations.literal: At fifteen he studied swordsmanship.
translations.idiomatic: At fifteen he took up fencing.

sentences.zh: 十六學《詩》、《書》，誦二十二萬言。

translations.literal: At sixteen he studied the Odes and the Documents, memorizing two hundred twenty thousand graphs.
translations.idiomatic: At sixteen he studied the Odes and Documents until he could recite two hundred twenty thousand characters.

sentences.zh: 十九學孫、吳兵法，戰陣之具，鉦鼓之教，亦誦二十二萬言。

translations.literal: At nineteen he studied the military methods of Sun and Wu—the gear of battle lines, the signals of gongs and drums—and likewise memorized two hundred twenty thousand graphs.
translations.idiomatic: At nineteen he mastered Sunzi and Wuzi on war—formations, gongs, and drums—and committed another two hundred twenty thousand characters to memory.

sentences.zh: 凡臣朔固已誦四十四萬言。

translations.literal: In all your servant Shuo has thus memorized four hundred forty thousand graphs.
translations.idiomatic: In sum I have four hundred forty thousand characters by heart.

sentences.zh: 又常服子路之言。

translations.literal: He also constantly takes to heart the words of Zilu.
translations.idiomatic: I try always to live by what Zilu taught.

sentences.zh: 臣朔年二十二，長九尺三寸，目若懸珠，齒若編貝，勇若孟賁，捷若慶忌，廉若鮑叔，信若尾生。

translations.literal: Your servant Shuo is twenty-two years old, nine feet three inches tall, with eyes like suspended pearls and teeth like strung shells, brave as Meng Ben, swift as Qingji, incorrupt as Bao Shu, trustworthy as Weisheng.
translations.idiomatic: I am twenty-two, stand nine feet three inches, my eyes shine like pearls and my teeth line up like shells; in courage I match Meng Ben, in agility Qingji, in integrity Bao Shu, in good faith Weisheng.

sentences.zh: 若此，可以為天子大臣矣。

translations.literal: If thus, he may serve as a great minister to the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: A man of such parts may stand as a minister to the Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 臣朔昧死再拜以聞。」

translations.literal: Your servant Shuo, in darkness of death, twice bows to report.
translations.idiomatic: I risk execution to lay this before you twice over.

sentences.zh: 朔文辭不遜，高自稱譽，上偉之，令待詔公車，奉祿薄，未得省見。

translations.literal: Shuo's phrasing was not deferential; he praised himself highly. The emperor marveled at him and ordered him to await edict at the Public Carriage office; his stipend was meager and he was not granted audience.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo's memorial was immodest and his self-praise extravagant, yet the emperor was impressed and rostered him at the Public Carriage office—meager pay and no audience in the inner palace.

sentences.zh: 久之，朔紿騶硃儒，曰：「上以若曹無益於縣官，耕田力作固不及人，臨眾處官不能治民，從軍擊虜不任兵事，無益於國用，徒索衣食，今欲盡殺若曹。」

translations.literal: After a long time Shuo deceived the stable runner Zhu Ru, saying: The emperor thinks you fellows do the exchequer no good—in farming and heavy labor you cannot match others, in facing crowds and holding office you cannot govern the people, in following the army to strike bandits you cannot bear military affairs; you benefit the state's budget not at all, only demand clothes and food—now he intends to kill you all.
translations.idiomatic: After long neglect Dongfang Shuo hoaxed the court dwarf Zhu Ru: His Majesty says you people are useless to the treasury—you cannot outwork farmers, cannot govern if given office, cannot fight if sent to the army; you only eat and wear what the state provides, so he means to execute the lot of you.

sentences.zh: 硃儒大恐，啼泣。

translations.literal: Zhu Ru was greatly terrified and wept.
translations.idiomatic: The dwarfs were panic-stricken and sobbed.

sentences.zh: 朔教曰：「上即過，叩頭請罪。」

translations.literal: Shuo instructed: When the emperor passes, knock your heads and beg forgiveness.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo told them, When he comes by, throw yourselves on the ground and plead for mercy.

sentences.zh: 居有頃，聞上過，硃儒皆號泣頓首。

translations.literal: Presently they heard the emperor passing; the Zhu Ru all wailed and knocked their heads.
translations.idiomatic: Soon they heard the imperial progress approaching and set up a chorus of weeping and kowtows.

sentences.zh: 上問：「何為？」

translations.literal: The emperor asked: What is this?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked what the matter was.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「東方朔言上欲盡誅臣等。」

translations.literal: They replied: Dongfang Shuo said Your Majesty wished to execute us all.
translations.idiomatic: They said Dongfang Shuo had told them the emperor planned to slaughter every dwarf.

sentences.zh: 上知朔多端，召問朔：「何恐硃儒為？」

translations.literal: The emperor, knowing Shuo had many stratagems, summoned and asked Shuo: Why did you frighten the Zhu Ru?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor knew Dongfang Shuo's tricks and called him in: Why did you terrify my dwarfs?

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣朔生亦言，死亦言。

translations.literal: He replied: Your servant Shuo speaks whether he lives or dies.
translations.idiomatic: He answered, I speak my mind in life and in death.

sentences.zh: 硃儒長三尺餘，奉一囊粟，錢二百四十。

translations.literal: The Zhu Ru are a little over three feet tall; they receive one sack of grain and two hundred forty cash.
translations.idiomatic: Those fellows stand a little over three feet high yet draw one full sack of grain and two hundred forty cash a month.

sentences.zh: 臣朔長九尺餘，亦奉一囊粟，錢二百四十。

translations.literal: Your servant Shuo is over nine feet tall, yet likewise receives one sack of grain and two hundred forty cash.
translations.idiomatic: I stand well over nine feet and am paid the same sack and the same two hundred forty cash.

sentences.zh: 硃儒飽欲死，臣朔饑欲死。

translations.literal: The Zhu Ru are so stuffed they could die; your servant Shuo is so hungry he could die.
translations.idiomatic: They are bloated to bursting while I starve on the same ration.

sentences.zh: 臣言可用，幸異其禮；

translations.literal: If your servant's words may be used, may you graciously alter his treatment;
translations.idiomatic: If my counsel is worth hearing, grant me a different scale of treatment;

sentences.zh: 不可用，罷之，無令但索長安米。」

translations.literal: if they may not be used, dismiss him—do not let him merely demand Chang'an's rice.
translations.idiomatic: if not, dismiss me and spare the capital's grain.

sentences.zh: 上大笑，因使待詔金馬門，稍得親近。

translations.literal: The emperor laughed greatly and thereupon had him await edict at the Golden Horse Gate; gradually he obtained closeness.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor roared with laughter, moved him to the Golden Horse Gate waiting list, and little by little drew him nearer the throne.

sentences.zh: 上嘗使諸數家射覆，置守宮盂下，射之，皆不能中。

translations.literal: The emperor once had the various diviners play shoot the cover: he placed a gecko under a bowl and had them guess; none could hit it.
translations.idiomatic: Once the emperor set the diviners a riddle: a gecko hidden under a jar. None could name it.

sentences.zh: 朔自讚曰：「臣嘗受《易》，請射之。」

translations.literal: Shuo himself volunteered, saying: Your servant has studied the Changes; allow him to shoot it.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo stepped forward: I have studied the Book of Changes; let me try.

sentences.zh: 乃別蓍布卦而對曰：「臣以為龍又無角，謂之為蛇又有足，跂跂脈脈善緣壁，是非守宮即蜥蜴。」

translations.literal: He then sorted the milfoil and spread the hexagrams and replied: Your servant thinks it is a dragon yet has no horns; call it a snake yet it has feet; creeping and pulsing it climbs walls well—if not a gecko then a lizard.
translations.idiomatic: He cast the stalks and said, It has not the dragon's horn nor the snake's limbless glide; it pads along the wall with a creeping gait—it must be a gecko or a wall lizard.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: Good.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, Well guessed.

sentences.zh: 賜帛十匹。

translations.literal: He granted ten bolts of silk.
translations.idiomatic: He rewarded him with ten bolts of silk.

sentences.zh: 復使射他物，連中，輒賜帛。

translations.literal: Again he had him shoot other objects; he hit in succession and each time was granted silk.
translations.idiomatic: Further riddles followed; each time Dongfang Shuo hit the mark and silk was piled into his hands.

sentences.zh: 時，有幸倡郭舍人，滑稽不窮，常侍左右，曰：「朔狂，幸中耳，非至數也。

translations.literal: At that time there was a favored entertainer, Guo Sheren, inexhaustibly witty, who constantly attended left and right, saying: Shuo is mad—he hit by luck, not by ultimate reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: A favorite court jester named Guo Sheren, quick with repartee, always at the sovereign's elbow, said, Dongfang Shuo is a madman; he guessed right by sheer luck, not by true numerology.

sentences.zh: 臣願令朔復射，朔中之，臣榜百，不能中，臣賜帛。」

translations.literal: Your servant wishes to have Shuo shoot again; if Shuo hits, your servant takes a hundred strokes; if he cannot hit, your servant is granted silk.
translations.idiomatic: Let him guess again: if he succeeds I take a hundred lashes; if he fails I claim the silk.

sentences.zh: 乃覆樹上寄生，令朔射之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they covered a tree parasite and ordered Shuo to shoot it.
translations.idiomatic: They hid a mistletoe sprig from a tree and challenged him to name it.

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「是寠藪也。」

translations.literal: Shuo said: It is a nest-thicket (wordplay).
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo said, It is a nest-thicket—a riddle-word for the parasite under the bowl.

sentences.zh: 舍人曰：「果知朔不能中也。」

translations.literal: Sheren said: As expected, I knew Shuo could not hit.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Sheren crowed, I knew he could not get this one.

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「生肉為膾，乾肉為脯；

translations.literal: Shuo said: Raw meat is mince; dried meat is jerky;
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo said, Raw meat sliced fine is kuai, dried meat is fu;

sentences.zh: 著樹為寄生，盆下為寠藪。」

translations.literal: on a tree it is mistletoe; under a bowl it is a nest-thicket.
translations.idiomatic: on a tree that growth is mistletoe; under a bowl the same thing is called nest-thicket.

sentences.zh: 上令倡監榜舍人，舍人不勝痛，呼□。

translations.literal: The emperor ordered the jester-supervisor to flog Sheren; Sheren could not bear the pain and cried out (text damaged).
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told the master of jesters to flog Guo Sheren until he howled with pain.

sentences.zh: 朔笑之曰：「咄！

translations.literal: Shuo laughed at him and said: Tut!
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo called out, Hoy!

sentences.zh: 口無毛，聲□，尻益高。」

translations.literal: No hair about the mouth, sound (lacuna), rump rising higher.
translations.idiomatic: No beard about the mouth, piping shrill as a nestling, haunches reared like a crane.

sentences.zh: 舍人恚曰：「朔擅詆欺天子從官，當棄市。」

translations.literal: Sheren angrily said: Shuo presumptuously slanders and deceives the Son of Heaven's attendants—he should be executed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Sheren snarled that Dongfang Shuo had publicly mocked an imperial attendant and deserved execution in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 上問朔：「何故詆之？」

translations.literal: The emperor asked Shuo: Why did you slander him?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked why he had insulted the man.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣非敢詆之，乃與為隱耳。」

translations.literal: He replied: Your servant did not dare slander him; he was playing a riddle with him.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo said, I did not insult him; I spoke in riddles.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「隱云何？」

translations.literal: The emperor said: What did the riddle mean?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked what the riddle meant.

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「夫口無毛者，狗竇也；

translations.literal: Shuo said: As for no hair about the mouth—it is a dog hole;
translations.idiomatic: No hair around the mouth is a dog's burrow;

sentences.zh: 聲□者，鳥哺□也；

translations.literal: As for sound (lacuna)—it is birds feeding (lacuna);
translations.idiomatic: A piping cry is fledglings begging in the nest;

sentences.zh: 尻益高者，鶴俯啄也。」

translations.literal: As for the rump rising higher—it is a crane bending to peck.
translations.idiomatic: Haunches lifted high is a crane stooping to strike.

sentences.zh: 舍人不服，因曰：「臣願復問朔隱語，不知，亦當榜。」

translations.literal: Sheren was not convinced and therefore said: Your servant wishes again to question Shuo with a riddle; if he does not know, he too should be flogged.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Sheren, still smarting, said, Let me set Dongfang Shuo another riddle—if he fails, he takes the rod in turn.

sentences.zh: 即妄為諧語曰：「令壺齟，老柏塗，伊優亞，狋吽牙。

translations.literal: He thereupon recklessly made a jesting phrase: Order pot crooked, old cypress smear, yi-you-ya, dog snarls.
translations.idiomatic: He spouted nonsense: Order—jar—crooked teeth; old—cypress—muddy path; yi-you-ya; mingled growls.

sentences.zh: 何謂也？」

translations.literal: What does it mean?
translations.idiomatic: What does that mean?

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「令者，命也。

translations.literal: Shuo said: Order means mandate.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo said, Order is command.

sentences.zh: 壺者，所以盛也。

translations.literal: Pot means that by which one contains.
translations.idiomatic: A jar is a vessel.

sentences.zh: 齟者，齒不正也。

translations.literal: Crooked means teeth not straight.
translations.idiomatic: Crooked teeth are snaggleteeth.

sentences.zh: 老者，人所敬也。

translations.literal: Old means what people revere.
translations.idiomatic: Age is venerable.

sentences.zh: 柏者，鬼之廷也。

translations.literal: Cypress means the court of ghosts.
translations.idiomatic: A cypress is the courtyard of ghosts—dark and forbidding.

sentences.zh: 塗者，漸洳徑也。

translations.literal: Smear means a soggy path.
translations.idiomatic: Mire is a boggy track.

sentences.zh: 伊優亞者，辭未定也。

translations.literal: Yi-you-ya means words not yet settled.
translations.idiomatic: Yi-you-ya is the babble of someone who cannot make up his mind.

sentences.zh: 狋吽牙者，兩犬爭也。」

translations.literal: Dog snarls means two dogs quarreling.
translations.idiomatic: The snarls are two dogs snapping at each other.

sentences.zh: 舍人所問，朔應聲輒對，變詐鋒出，莫能窮者，左右大驚。

translations.literal: To whatever Sheren asked, Shuo answered on the instant; shifts and tricks poured sharp as a blade—none could exhaust him; those left and right were greatly amazed.
translations.idiomatic: Whatever Guo Sheren threw at him, Dongfang Shuo answered in the same breath with wit that never flagged; the whole court stared.

sentences.zh: 上以朔為常侍郎，遂得愛幸。

translations.literal: The emperor made Shuo a regular attendant gentleman, and he thereupon obtained favor.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named him a regular palace gentleman, and from then on he basked in imperial favor.

sentences.zh: 久之，伏日，詔賜從官肉。

translations.literal: After a long time, on the dog-day festival, an edict ordered meat granted to the attendants.
translations.idiomatic: On a midsummer dog-day the emperor ordered meat distributed to his attendants.

sentences.zh: 大官丞日晏下來，朔獨拔劍割肉，謂其同官曰：「伏日當蚤歸，請受賜。」

translations.literal: The grand provisioner's assistant came down late in the day; Shuo alone drew his sword and carved meat, saying to his fellow officials: On dog-days one should return early—allow me to accept the gift.
translations.idiomatic: The steward's deputy arrived near evening; Dongfang Shuo alone whipped out his sword, carved his share, and told his colleagues, Dog-day leave is short—I am taking my portion home.

sentences.zh: 即懷肉去。

translations.literal: He thereupon tucked the meat in his bosom and left.
translations.idiomatic: He stuffed the meat in his robe and walked off.

sentences.zh: 大官奏之。

translations.literal: The grand provisioner reported him.
translations.idiomatic: The steward reported him.

sentences.zh: 朔入，上曰：「昨賜肉，不待詔，以劍割肉而去之，何也？」

translations.literal: When Shuo entered, the emperor said: Yesterday when meat was granted, without awaiting edict you carved meat with a sword and left—why?
translations.idiomatic: When Dongfang Shuo was brought in, the emperor said, Yesterday I gave out meat; you carved yours with a sword and left without waiting for my word—why?

sentences.zh: 朔免冠謝。

translations.literal: Shuo removed his cap and apologized.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo doffed his cap and apologized.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先生起，自責也！」

translations.literal: The emperor said: Sir, rise—blame yourself!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, Stand up, Master, and blame yourself!

sentences.zh: 朔再拜曰：「朔來！

translations.literal: Shuo twice bowed and said: Shuo comes!
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo bowed twice and cried, Shuo, come forward!

sentences.zh: 朔來！

translations.literal: Shuo comes!
translations.idiomatic: Shuo, come forward!

sentences.zh: 受賜不待詔，何無禮也！

translations.literal: Accepting a gift without awaiting edict—how lacking in propriety!
translations.idiomatic: To take a gift without the emperor's word—how rude!

sentences.zh: 拔劍割肉，一何壯也！

translations.literal: Drawing a sword to carve meat—how bold!
translations.idiomatic: To carve meat with your own blade—how bold!

sentences.zh: 割之不多，又何廉也！

translations.literal: Carving not much—how incorrupt again!
translations.idiomatic: To take so modest a slice—how temperate!

sentences.zh: 歸遺細君，又何仁也！」

translations.literal: Taking it home to give the little lord—how humane again!
translations.idiomatic: To carry it home to your wife—how tender-hearted!

sentences.zh: 上笑曰：「使先生自責，乃反自譽！」

translations.literal: The emperor laughed and said: Having you blame yourself, you instead praise yourself!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor laughed and said, I asked you to blame yourself, and you have praised yourself instead!

sentences.zh: 復賜酒一石，肉百斤，歸遺細君。

translations.literal: He again granted a stone of wine and a hundred jin of meat to take home to the little lord.
translations.idiomatic: He sent him another stone of wine and a hundred jin of meat for his wife.

sentences.zh: 初，建元三年，微行始出，北至池陽，西至黃山，南獵長楊，東遊宜春。

translations.literal: At first, in the third year of Jianyuan, incognito outings began: north to Chiyang, west to Huangshan, south to hunt at Changyang, east to roam Yichun.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianyuan 3 the emperor began slipping out in disguise—north to Chiyang, west to Huangshan, south to hunt at Changyang, east to sport at Yichun.

sentences.zh: 微行常用飲酎已。

translations.literal: Incognito outings commonly used the drinking of the autumn ale as their finish.
translations.idiomatic: These secret jaunts usually waited until after the ritual tasting of the new ale.

sentences.zh: 八九月中，與侍中常侍武騎及待詔隴西北地良家子能騎射者期諸殿門，故有「期門」之號自此始。

translations.literal: In the eighth or ninth month, together with palace attendants, regular attendants, military riders, and awaiting-edict good-family sons of Longxi and Beidi who could ride and shoot, he would rendezvous at the palace gates—therefore the title Gate Rendezvous began from this.
translations.idiomatic: In the eighth or ninth lunar months he would meet picked household guards, palace attendants, and crack horsemen from Longxi and Beidi at the palace gates—hence the corps took the name Gate Rendezvous.

sentences.zh: 微行以夜漏下十刻乃出，常稱平陽侯。

translations.literal: Incognito outings waited until the clepsydra had dropped ten night marks before setting out; he always called himself Marquis of Pingyang.
translations.idiomatic: He left only after the tenth night watch and passed himself off as the marquis of Pingyang.

sentences.zh: 旦明，入山下馳射鹿豕狐兔，手格熊羆，馳騖禾稼稻粳之地。

translations.literal: At dawn, entering the hills below he galloped and shot deer, boar, fox, and hare, wrestled bears with his hands, and raced through fields of grain and rice.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak he would gallop through the foothills shooting deer, boar, fox, and hare, grapple bears with his bare hands, and thunder across standing grain.

sentences.zh: 民皆號呼罵詈，相聚會，自言鄠杜令。

translations.literal: The people all shouted and cursed, gathered in meetings, and declared themselves the magistrates of Hu and Du.
translations.idiomatic: Farmers howled abuse, banded together, and claimed to be the magistrates of Hu and Du.

sentences.zh: 令往，欲謁平陽侯，諸騎欲擊鞭之。

translations.literal: When a magistrate went, wishing to pay court to the Marquis of Pingyang, the riders wished to strike him with whips.
translations.idiomatic: When a real magistrate came to call on the marquis, the escort wanted to whip him away.

sentences.zh: 令大怒。

translations.literal: The magistrate was greatly angered.
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate exploded with rage.

sentences.zh: 使吏呵止，獵者數騎見留，乃示以乘輿物，久之乃得去。

translations.literal: He sent clerks to shout and stop them; several riders of the hunt party were detained until he showed them imperial carriage gear; only after a long time could they leave.
translations.idiomatic: He sent runners to intervene; a few of the hunting party were held until he produced tokens of the imperial equipage, and only then were they allowed to go.

sentences.zh: 時夜出夕還，後繼五日糧，會朝長信官，上大歡樂之。

translations.literal: At that time he went out by night and returned at evening, carrying five days' rations behind him; he would attend court at the Changle Palace— the emperor greatly delighted in it.
translations.idiomatic: He would ride out at night and return at dusk with five days' rations, then appear at audience in the Changle Palace—the emperor loved the sport.

sentences.zh: 是後，南山下乃知微行數出也，然尚迫於太后，未敢遠出。

translations.literal: After this, south of Mount Zhong those below knew incognito outings went out repeatedly, yet still pressed by the empress dowager, he did not dare go far.
translations.idiomatic: Word spread south of the capital that the emperor was often abroad in disguise, but the empress dowager still kept him from roaming too far.

sentences.zh: 丞相御史知指，乃使右輔都尉徼循長楊以東，右內史發小民共待會所。

translations.literal: The chancellor and Imperial Counselor grasped the intent and thereupon sent the right assistant metropolitan commandant to patrol east of Changyang, and the right metropolitan superintendent to mobilize common folk to await together at the rendezvous points.
translations.idiomatic: Reading the signs, the chancellor and censor ordered the right-flank commandant to patrol east of Changyang and the right metropolitan superintendent to draft peasants to line the emperor's routes.

sentences.zh: 後乃私置更衣，從宣曲以南十二所，中休更衣，投宿諸宮，長楊、五柞、倍陽、宣曲尤幸。

translations.literal: Afterward he privately set up changing rooms—twelve places from Xuqu south—resting midway to change clothes, lodging overnight in the various palaces; Changyang, Wuzuo, Beiyang, and Xuqu were especially favored.
translations.idiomatic: Later he had a dozen discreet lodges built south of Xuqu where he could change dress, rest, and sleep over at Changyang, Wuzuo, Beiyang, and Xuqu—his favorite haunts.

sentences.zh: 於是上以為道遠勞苦，又為百姓所患，乃使太中大夫吾丘壽王與待詔能用算者二人，舉籍阿城以南，□以東，宜春以西，提封頃畝，乃其賈直，欲除以為上林苑，屬之南山。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor, thinking the way was distant and toilsome and also a trouble to the common people, sent Grand Counsellor Wuqiu Shouwang together with two awaiting-edict men skilled in calculation to raise the registers from south of Acheng, east of (lacuna), west of Yichun, the sealed field acreage and their purchase values, wishing to set these aside to make the Shanglin Park, joining it to the Southern Mountains.
translations.idiomatic: Weary of the long rides and the havoc among the people, he sent Wuqiu Shouwang and two mathematicians on the waiting roster to survey every registered field from south of Acheng eastward (one toponym is missing in the text) to west of Yichun, tally acreage and price, and annex the tract as the Shanglin hunting park running to the Southern Mountains.

sentences.zh: 又詔中尉、左右內史表屬縣草田，欲以償鄠杜之民。

translations.literal: He also edicted the commandant of the capital and the left and right metropolitan superintendents to list waste fields of subordinate counties, wishing to use them to compensate the people of Hu and Du.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered the capital commandant and the metropolitan superintendents to list idle public land in their counties as compensation for the farmers of Hu and Du.

sentences.zh: 吾丘壽王奏事，上大說稱善。

translations.literal: When Wuqiu Shouwang reported on the affair, the emperor was greatly pleased and praised it.
translations.idiomatic: Wuqiu Shouwang's report delighted the emperor.

sentences.zh: 時朔在傍，進諫曰：

translations.literal: At that time Shuo was at his side and advanced remonstrance, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo, standing by, offered remonstrance:

sentences.zh: 臣聞謙遜靜愨，天表之應，應之以福；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that modesty, yielding, quietness, and sincerity are Heaven's outward signs; responding to them brings blessing;
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that humility and quiet reverence draw Heaven's favor and bring good fortune;

sentences.zh: 驕溢靡麗，天表之應，應之以異。

translations.literal: arrogance, overflow, dissipation, and splendor are Heaven's outward signs; responding to them brings prodigies.
translations.idiomatic: while arrogance and wanton luxury draw Heaven's warning in strange portents.

sentences.zh: 今陛下累郎台，恐其不高也；

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty piles terrace on terrace, fearing they are not high enough;
translations.idiomatic: You raise tower upon tower, as though none were high enough;

sentences.zh: 弋獵之處，恐其不廣也。

translations.literal: and at the places of archery and hunting, fearing they are not broad enough.
translations.idiomatic: and you widen the hunting grounds as though no park were ever broad enough.

sentences.zh: 如天不為變，則三輔之地盡可以為苑，何必盩厔、鄠、杜乎！

translations.literal: If Heaven did not make prodigies for it, then the lands of the three assistants could all be made into parks—why must it be Zhouzhi, Hu, and Du!
translations.idiomatic: If Heaven raised no omens against it, you could turn the entire capital region into a hunting park—why stop at Zhouzhi, Hu, and Du?

sentences.zh: 奢侈越制，天為之變，上林雖小，臣尚以為大也。

translations.literal: Extravagance exceeding regulation—Heaven makes prodigies for it; though Shanglin is small, your servant still thinks it large.
translations.idiomatic: Extravagance beyond the rites draws Heaven's rebuke; even the existing Shanglin seems to me already too vast.

sentences.zh: 夫南山，天下之阻也，南有江、淮，北有河、渭，其地從汧、隴以東，商、雒以西，厥壤肥饒。

translations.literal: As for the Southern Mountains, they are the barrier of the realm; south are the Yangzi and Huai, north are the Yellow River and Wei; the land from east of Qian and Long to west of Shang and Luo—its soil is fat and rich.
translations.idiomatic: The Southern Mountains wall off the heart of the empire: the Yangzi and Huai lie to the south, the Yellow River and Wei to the north, and from the Long foothills east to the Shangluo uplands the soil is famously rich.

sentences.zh: 漢興，去三河之地，止霸、產以西，都涇、渭之南，此所謂天下陸海之地，秦之所以虜西戎兼山東者也。

translations.literal: When Han rose, it left the land of the Three Rivers, stopped west of Ba and Chan, and capitalized south of the Jing and Wei—this is what is called the realm's dry sea, whereby Qin captured the western Rong and swallowed Shandong.
translations.idiomatic: Our dynasty abandoned the central plain, drew its capital west of the Ba and Chan rivers south of the Jing and Wei—the dry land sea that let Qin subdue the western tribes and conquer the east.

sentences.zh: 其山出玉石，金、銀、銅、鐵，豫章、檀、柘，異類之物，不可勝原，此百工所取給，萬民所卬足也。

translations.literal: Its mountains yield jade and stone, gold, silver, copper, and iron, camphor, sandalwood, and mulberry—things of strange kinds cannot be fully traced; this is what the hundred crafts draw supplies from, what the myriad people look up to for sufficiency.
translations.idiomatic: Those hills yield jade, metals, and fine timber beyond counting—the industrial base of the workshops and the livelihood of the common people.

sentences.zh: 又有粳稻、梨、栗、桑、麻、竹箭之饒，土宜姜芋，水多蛙魚，貧者得以人給家足，無饑寒之憂。

translations.literal: Moreover there is abundance of glutinous rice, pears, chestnuts, mulberry, hemp, and bamboo arrows; the soil suits ginger and taro; the waters hold many frogs and fish; the poor can thereby have each person and household sufficient, without worry of hunger and cold.
translations.idiomatic: There are rice, fruit, silk, hemp, and bamboo in plenty; ginger and taro thrive in the soil, frogs and fish teem in the streams, so even the poor can feed their families without fear of hunger or cold.

sentences.zh: 故□、鎬之間號為土膏，其賈畝一金。

translations.literal: Therefore between (lacuna) and Hao it is called soil ointment; its price per mu is one jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Between Feng and Hao the earth is so prized that fields once fetched an ounce of gold per mu. (One character is missing in the text.)

sentences.zh: 今規以為苑，絕陂池水澤之利，而取民膏腴之地，上乏國家之用，下奪農桑之業，棄成功，就敗事，損耗五穀，是其不可一也。

translations.literal: Now planning to make it a park cuts off the benefit of ponds, pools, water, and marshes, and seizes the people's fat fertile land—above it depletes what the state uses, below it seizes farming and mulberry work, abandons completed success and pursues failed affairs, wastes and consumes the five grains—this is its unacceptability, first.
translations.idiomatic: To ring it off as a park would dry up irrigation, seize the richest farms, drain the treasury above and destroy farming below—throwing away a sure thing for a folly. That is my first objection.

sentences.zh: 且盛荊棘之林，而長養麋鹿，廣狐兔之苑，大虎狼之虛，又壞人塚墓，發人室廬，令幼弱懷土而思，耆老泣涕而悲，是其不可二也。

translations.literal: Moreover raising thickets of brambles and thorns and growing elk and deer, widening preserves for foxes and hares, enlarging wastes for tigers and wolves, again destroying people's tombs and mounds, tearing up people's houses and huts, making the young and weak cling to the soil and pine, the old weep tears and grieve—this is its unacceptability, second.
translations.idiomatic: You would turn farms into bramble patches for deer, foxes, and wolves, tear down tombs and homes, and send children and elders into exile weeping. That is my second objection.

sentences.zh: 斥而營之，垣而囿之，騎馳東西，車騖南北，又有深溝大渠，夫一日之樂不足以危無堤之輿，是其不可三也。

translations.literal: Clearing and encamping it, walling and enclosing it, riders galloping east and west, chariots racing north and south, again having deep ditches and great channels—a single day's pleasure is not worth risking an unwalled carriage—this is its unacceptability, third.
translations.idiomatic: Deep ditches, racing chariots, and a day's sport are not worth imperiling the ruler's life and limb. That is my third objection.

sentences.zh: 故務苑囿之大，不恤農時，非所以強國富人也。

translations.literal: Therefore striving for the greatness of parks and preserves without pitying the farming season is not the means to strengthen the state and enrich the people.
translations.idiomatic: To chase ever larger parks while ignoring the farming calendar is no way to enrich state or people.

sentences.zh: 夫殷作九市之宮而諸侯畔，靈王起章華之台而楚民散，秦興阿房之殿而天下亂。

translations.literal: Yin built the palace of the nine markets and the feudal lords rebelled; King Ling raised the Zhanghua terrace and the people of Chu scattered; Qin raised the Epang hall and the realm was in chaos.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhou of Yin built his pleasure tower and the lords turned against him; King Ling of Chu raised Zhanghua and his people fled; Qin built Epang and the empire fell apart.

sentences.zh: 糞土愚臣，忘生觸死，逆盛意，犯隆指，罪當萬死，不勝大願，願陳《泰階六符》，以觀天變，不可不省。

translations.literal: Mud-and-dung foolish minister, forgetting life and touching death, opposing flourishing intent, offending lofty direction—crime fits ten thousand deaths; unable to overcome a great wish, wishes to present the Six Talismans of the Grand Steps to observe Heaven's changes—they cannot but be examined.
translations.idiomatic: I am dust beneath your feet, yet I risk death to oppose your will. I deserve execution a thousand times over, but I beg to present the Six Talismans of the Grand Steps so you may read Heaven's warnings—they merit your attention.

sentences.zh: 是日因奏《泰階》之事，上乃拜朔為太中大夫給事中，賜黃金百斤。

translations.literal: That day he thereupon presented the matter of the Grand Steps; the emperor then appointed Shuo Grand Counsellor with Palace Attendance and granted a hundred jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: That day he lectured on the Grand Steps; the emperor named him Grand Counsellor with access to the inner palace and sent a hundred jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 然遂起上林苑，如壽王所奏雲。

translations.literal: Yet thereupon Shanglin Park was raised, as Shouwang had memorialized.
translations.idiomatic: The Shanglin Park was built anyway, just as Wuqiu Shouwang had proposed.

sentences.zh: 久之，隆慮公主子昭平君尚帝女夷安公主，隆慮主病困，以金千斤、錢千萬為昭平君豫贖死罪，上許之。

translations.literal: After a long time, the Princess of Longlü's son Zhao Pingjun married the emperor's daughter Princess Yi'an; when the Princess of Longlü was gravely ill, with a thousand jin of gold and ten million cash she prepaid a ransom for Zhao Pingjun's death crime; the emperor assented.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Pingjun, son of the Princess of Longlü, married Princess Yi'an. On her deathbed his mother deposited a thousand pounds of gold and ten million cash to ransom any capital crime he might commit, and the emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 隆慮主卒，昭平君日驕，醉殺主傅，獄系內宮。

translations.literal: When the Princess of Longlü died, Zhao Pingjun daily grew arrogant; drunk, he killed the princess's tutor and was bound in prison in the inner palace.
translations.idiomatic: After her death Zhao Pingjun grew insolent, slew his tutor in a drunken rage, and was jailed in the inner palace.

sentences.zh: 以公主子，廷尉上請請論。

translations.literal: Because he was a princess's son, the commandant of justice submitted a request asking for judgment.
translations.idiomatic: As a princess's son the case went to the commandant of justice for sentence.

sentences.zh: 左右人人為言：「前又入贖，陛下許之。」

translations.literal: Those left and right all spoke for him: Before he also entered ransom; Your Majesty permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: Every attendant pleaded, His mother paid the ransom in advance, and you granted it.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「吾弟老有是一子，死以屬我。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: My younger sister in old age had this one son; dying she entrusted him to me.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, My sister in her old age had only this son; with her dying breath she placed him in my care.

sentences.zh: 於是為之垂涕歎息良久，曰：「法令者，先帝所造也，用弟故而誣先帝之法，吾何面目入高廟乎！

translations.literal: Thereupon for him he shed tears and sighed long, saying: Laws and orders are what the former emperor established; because of a younger sibling's sake to violate the former emperor's laws—what face have I to enter the High Temple!
translations.idiomatic: He wept and sighed a long while, then said, Law is the legacy of the late emperor. To break it for my sister's sake—how could I face the ancestors in the High Temple?

sentences.zh: 又下負萬民。」

translations.literal: Again below I would betray the myriad people.
translations.idiomatic: How could I face the people below?

sentences.zh: 乃可其奏，哀不能自止，左右盡悲。

translations.literal: Thereupon he approved the memorial; grief could not stop itself; those left and right were all sorrowful.
translations.idiomatic: He approved the death sentence, wept uncontrollably, and the whole court wept with him.

sentences.zh: 朔前上壽，曰：「臣聞聖王為政，賞不避仇讎，誅不擇骨肉。

translations.literal: Shuo stepped forward with a longevity toast, saying: Your servant has heard that when a sage king conducts government, rewards do not avoid enemies, punishments do not pick kin.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo stepped up with a toast: The sage king rewards even his foes and punishes even his kin.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰：『不偏不黨，王道蕩蕩。』

translations.literal: The Documents say: "Without partiality, without faction—the kingly way is vast."
translations.idiomatic: The Documents say, Without favor, without clique, the royal way runs straight and wide.

sentences.zh: 此二者，五帝所重，三王所難也。

translations.literal: These two things are what the Five Emperors esteemed and what the Three Kings found hard.
translations.idiomatic: Both precepts were honored by the Five Emperors and strained even the Three Kings.

sentences.zh: 陛下行之，是以四海之內元元之民各得其所，天下幸甚！

translations.literal: Your Majesty practices them—therefore within the four seas the black-haired people each obtain their place; the realm is greatly fortunate!
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty has done this: every commoner in the realm finds justice, and the empire is the better for it.

sentences.zh: 臣朔奉觴，昧死再拜上萬歲壽。」

translations.literal: Your servant Shuo raises the cup, in darkness of death twice bows and offers the myriad-years longevity.
translations.idiomatic: I raise my cup and wish Your Majesty ten thousand years of life.

sentences.zh: 上乃起，入省中，夕時召讓朔，曰：「傳曰『時然後言，人不厭其言』。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon rose, entered the private chambers; at evening he summoned and rebuked Shuo, saying: The tradition says, "When it is timely then speak—people do not tire of his words."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor withdrew, then at dusk called Dongfang Shuo in to scold him: The classic says, Speak only when the moment is right, and men will not weary of your words.

sentences.zh: 今先生上壽，時乎？」

translations.literal: Now, sir, your longevity toast—was it timely?
translations.idiomatic: Was your toast today timely?

sentences.zh: 朔免冠頓首曰：「臣聞樂太盛則陽溢，哀太盛則陰損，陰陽變則心氣動，心氣動則精神散，精神散而邪氣及。

translations.literal: Shuo removed his cap and knocked his head, saying: Your servant has heard that when joy is too great yang overflows; when grief is too great yin is harmed; when yin and yang shift, heart qi moves; when heart qi moves, spirit scatters; when spirit scatters, evil qi arrives.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo kowtowed bareheaded: Extreme joy floods the yang ether; extreme grief drains the yin. When yin and yang reel, the heart loses its anchor, the spirit frays, and evil humors invade the body.

sentences.zh: 銷憂者莫若酒，臣朔所以上壽者，明陛下正而不阿，因以止哀也。

translations.literal: Nothing dispels worry like wine; the reason your servant offered the longevity toast was to show Your Majesty is upright and not partial, and thereby to stop grief.
translations.idiomatic: Wine best drives care away. I raised a cup to show that Your Majesty holds straight to justice without favoritism, and to help you set grief aside.

sentences.zh: 愚不知忌諱，當死。」

translations.literal: Stupidly not knowing taboo, I deserve death.
translations.idiomatic: I spoke bluntly and deserve death.

sentences.zh: 先是，朔嘗醉入殿中，小遺殿上，劾不敬。

translations.literal: Before this, Shuo had once entered the hall drunk and urinated a little on the hall; he was impeached for irreverence.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, drunk in the palace, he had relieved himself on the hall floor and was charged with lèse-majesté.

sentences.zh: 有詔免為庶人，待詔宦者署。

translations.literal: An edict ordered him demoted to commoner status, awaiting edict at the eunuchs' office.
translations.idiomatic: An edict reduced him to commoner rank on the eunuchs' office waiting list.

sentences.zh: 因此對復為中郎，賜帛百匹。

translations.literal: Because of this reply he was again made gentleman of the palace and granted a hundred bolts of silk.
translations.idiomatic: After this exchange he was restored as gentleman of the palace and given a hundred bolts of silk.

sentences.zh: 初，帝姑館陶公主號竇太主，堂邑侯陳午尚之。

translations.literal: At first, the emperor's aunt the Princess of Guantao, titled the Grand Dowager Dou's daughter, was married by Marquis of Tangyi Chen Wu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's aunt, the Princess of Guantao—known as the Elder Princess Dou—had married Chen Wu, the marquis of Tangyi.

sentences.zh: 午死，主寡居，年五十餘矣，近幸董偃。

translations.literal: When Wu died, the princess dwelt a widow, being over fifty years old, and drew near and favored Dong Yan.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Wu died she was past fifty, a widow who took Dong Yan as her lover.

sentences.zh: 始偃與母以賣珠為事，偃年十三，隨母出入主家。

translations.literal: At first Yan and his mother made their living selling pearls; at thirteen Yan followed his mother in and out of the princess's house.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan and his mother had sold pearls for a living; at thirteen he began following his mother to the princess's mansion.

sentences.zh: 左右言其姣好，主召見，曰；

translations.literal: Those about spoke of his handsome looks; the princess summoned and saw him, saying;
translations.idiomatic: Her attendants praised the boy's looks; she called him in and said,

sentences.zh: 「吾為母養之。」

translations.literal: I will raise him for his mother.
translations.idiomatic: I will adopt him and raise him for his mother.

sentences.zh: 因留第中，教書計相馬御射，頗讀傳記。

translations.literal: Thereupon she kept him in the mansion, teaching accounts, judging horses, riding and archery; he read somewhat of chronicles and records.
translations.idiomatic: She kept him in her household, taught him reckoning, horsemanship, and archery, and had him read histories and tales.

sentences.zh: 至年十八而冠，出則執轡，入則侍內。

translations.literal: When he reached eighteen he capped; going out he held the reins, entering he attended within.
translations.idiomatic: At his capping at eighteen he led her carriage abroad and attended her within.

sentences.zh: 為人溫柔愛人，以主故，諸公接之，名稱城中，號曰董君。

translations.literal: In person he was gentle and warm and loved people; because of the princess the great lords received him; his name was praised in the capital; he was called Lord Dong.
translations.idiomatic: Courteous and affable, he was received by the nobility for the princess's sake and was known through the capital as Lord Dong.

sentences.zh: 主因推令散財交士，令中府曰：「董君所發，一日金滿百斤，錢滿百萬，帛滿千匹，乃白之。」

translations.literal: The princess thereupon urged him to scatter wealth and befriend knights, ordering the inner treasury: Whatever Lord Dong disburses—if in one day gold fills a hundred jin, cash fills a million, silk fills a thousand bolts, then report it.
translations.idiomatic: She urged him to spend freely and win friends, telling the treasury not to bother her unless he drew more than a hundred jin of gold, a million cash, or a thousand bolts of silk in a single day.

sentences.zh: 安陵爰叔者，爰盎兄子也，與偃善，謂偃曰：「足下私侍漢主，挾不測之罪，將欲安處乎？」

translations.literal: Yuanshu of Anling was the nephew of Yuan Ang; he was on good terms with Yan and said to Yan: You privately serve the Han ruler, bearing immeasurable crime—where do you intend to end up?
translations.idiomatic: Yuanshu of Anling, Yuan Ang's nephew and Dong Yan's friend, warned him, You are the princess's secret favorite—a capital offense. What is your long-term plan?

sentences.zh: 偃懼曰：「憂之久矣，不知所以。」

translations.literal: Yan fearfully said: I have worried long; I do not know what to do.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan said, I have worried for ages but see no way out.

sentences.zh: 爰叔曰：「顧城廟遠無宿宮，又有萩竹籍田，足下何不白主獻長門園？

translations.literal: Yuanshu said: Consider that the suburban altars are far and have no lodging palace, and there is also reed bamboo and the ritual field—why do you not report to the princess to offer the Changmen garden?
translations.idiomatic: Yuanshu said, The emperor lacks a lodge near the suburban altars and must cross the imperial field. Why not have her deed the Changmen park to the throne?

sentences.zh: 此上所欲也。

translations.literal: This is what the superior desires.
translations.idiomatic: That is exactly what he wants.

sentences.zh: 如是，上知計出於足下也，則安枕而臥，長無慘怛之憂。

translations.literal: If so, when the superior knows the plan came from your feet, then he will pillow peacefully and sleep, and long have no bitter grief.
translations.idiomatic: He will know the gift was your idea, sleep without fear, and cease to brood on silencing you.

sentences.zh: 久之不然，上且請之，於足下何如？」

translations.literal: If after long it is not so, the superior will ask for it—how will that be for your feet?
translations.idiomatic: Delay, and the emperor may take it by force—what then for you?

sentences.zh: 偃頓首曰：「敬奉教。」

translations.literal: Yan knocked his head and said: Respectfully I receive your teaching.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan kowtowed and said, I shall do as you say.

sentences.zh: 入言之主，主立奏書獻之。

translations.literal: He entered and spoke to the princess; she immediately submitted a memorial presenting it.
translations.idiomatic: He told the princess; she memorialized the offer at once.

sentences.zh: 上大說，更名竇大主園為長門宮。

translations.literal: The emperor was greatly pleased and renamed the Grand Elder Princess Dou's garden Changmen Palace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was delighted and renamed her estate the Changmen Palace.

sentences.zh: 主大喜，使偃以黃金百斤為爰叔壽。

translations.literal: The princess was greatly pleased and sent Yan with a hundred jin of gold to toast Yuanshu's longevity.
translations.idiomatic: Overjoyed, she sent Dong Yan with a hundred jin of gold to thank Yuanshu.

sentences.zh: 叔因是為董君畫求見上之策，令主稱疾不朝。

translations.literal: Yuanshu thereupon for Lord Dong plotted the plan of seeking audience with the emperor, ordering the princess to claim illness and not attend court.
translations.idiomatic: Yuanshu then arranged Dong Yan's introduction: the princess was to feign illness and skip court.

sentences.zh: 上往臨疾，問所欲，主辭謝曰：「妾幸蒙陛下厚恩，先帝遺德，奉朝請之禮，備臣妾之儀，列為公主，賞賜邑入，隆天重地，死無以塞責。

translations.literal: The emperor went to visit her illness and asked what she wished; the princess declined with thanks, saying: Your handmaid has fortunately received Your Majesty's thick grace and the former emperor's bequeathed virtue, observed the rite of court attendance, fulfilled the deportment of subject and concubine, been ranked a princess, been granted fief income—heaven high, earth heavy—in death I have no means to repay the obligation.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor called, she said, I owe Your Majesty and the late emperor more than heaven and earth could repay; though I serve as a princess I cannot discharge my debt even in death.

sentences.zh: 一日卒有不勝灑掃之職，先狗馬填溝壑，竊有所恨，不勝大願，願陛下時忘萬事，養精游神，從中掖庭回輿，枉路臨妾山林，得獻觴上壽，娛樂左右。

translations.literal: If one day I suddenly am unequal to the duty of sprinkling and sweeping, before the dogs and horses I fill ditches and gullies—privately I have a regret, unable to overcome a great wish: I wish Your Majesty from time to time to forget the myriad affairs, nurture essence and roam spirit, turn the carriage from the central palace lanes, bend the road to visit your handmaid's mountain grove, be able to raise a cup and offer longevity, and delight those left and right.
translations.idiomatic: If I should die before repaying you, one wish would remain: that you set the state aside awhile, refresh your spirit, turn your carriage from the palace, and deign to visit my poor villa so I may pour wine for you and cheer your attendants.

sentences.zh: 如是而死，何恨之有！」

translations.literal: If I die thus, what regret would there be!
translations.idiomatic: Then I could die content.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「主何憂？

translations.literal: The emperor said: What does the princess worry about?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, Why such gloom?

sentences.zh: 幸得愈。

translations.literal: Fortunately you will recover.
translations.idiomatic: You will recover.

sentences.zh: 恐群臣從官多，大為主費。」

translations.literal: I fear the many ministers and followers would greatly cost the princess.
translations.idiomatic: I only fear a full train of officials would burden you with expense.

sentences.zh: 上還，有頃，主疾愈，起謁，上以錢千萬從主飲。

translations.literal: The emperor returned; after a while the princess's illness improved; she rose and paid court; the emperor brought ten million cash to drink with the princess.
translations.idiomatic: Soon she recovered and attended court; the emperor brought ten million cash to drink with her.

sentences.zh: 後數日，上臨山林，主自執宰敝膝，道入登階就坐。

translations.literal: Several days later the emperor visited the mountain grove; the princess herself took the cook's worn apron, guided him in, ascended the steps, and took seat.
translations.idiomatic: Days later he visited her estate; she met him in a cook's apron like a common hostess, led him in, and seated him.

sentences.zh: 坐未定，上曰：「願謁主人翁。」

translations.literal: Before seating was settled, the emperor said: I wish to greet the master of the house.
translations.idiomatic: Before they had settled, he said, I would meet the man of the house.

sentences.zh: 主乃下殿，去簪珥，徒跣頓首謝曰：「妾無狀，負陛下，身當伏誅。

translations.literal: The princess thereupon descended the hall, removed hairpins and earrings, went barefoot and knocked her head in apology, saying: Your handmaid has behaved disgracefully and failed Your Majesty; her person should suffer execution.
translations.idiomatic: She slipped from the dais, stripped her ornaments, and kowtowed barefoot: I have shamed you and deserve death.

sentences.zh: 陛下不致之法，頓首死罪。」

translations.literal: Your Majesty has not applied the law to me—I knock my head for a capital crime.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you have not held me to the law—I kowtow for my capital crime.

sentences.zh: 有詔謝。

translations.literal: There was an edict to excuse her.
translations.idiomatic: An edict bade her rise.

sentences.zh: 主簪履起，之東廂自引董君。

translations.literal: The princess pinned hair and donned shoes, rose, went to the east wing and herself led forth Lord Dong.
translations.idiomatic: She dressed again, went to the east wing, and led Dong Yan out.

sentences.zh: 董君綠幘傅□，隨主前，伏殿下。

translations.literal: Lord Dong in green headcloth and (lacuna) attached, followed the princess forward and prostrated below the hall.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan wore the green turban of a servant and jeweled sleeves; he followed her forward and knelt below the steps.

sentences.zh: 主乃讚：「館陶公主胞人臣偃昧死再拜謁。」

translations.literal: The princess then announced: The Princess of Guantao's chamberlain, your subject Yan, in darkness of death twice bows to pay court.
translations.idiomatic: She announced, Your servant Dong Yan, private attendant of the Princess of Guantao, kowtows in fear of death.

sentences.zh: 因叩頭謝，上為之起。

translations.literal: Thereupon he knocked his head in thanks; the emperor for him rose.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan kowtowed; the emperor rose for him.

sentences.zh: 有詔賜衣冠上。

translations.literal: An edict ordered court dress granted and he ascend.
translations.idiomatic: An edict summoned him to don proper cap and robes.

sentences.zh: 偃起，走就衣冠。

translations.literal: Yan rose and hastened to take the cap and robes.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan rose and went to change.

sentences.zh: 主自奉食進觴。

translations.literal: The princess herself served food and advanced cups.
translations.idiomatic: The princess herself poured his wine and served the meal.

sentences.zh: 當是時，董君見尊不名，稱為「主人翁」，飲大歡樂。

translations.literal: At that time Lord Dong was honored without being named; he was called master of the house; drinking was greatly joyful.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan was toasted as master of the house rather than by name, and the banquet grew merry.

sentences.zh: 主乃請賜將軍、列侯、從官金錢雜繒各有數。

translations.literal: The princess then requested grants of gold, cash, and assorted silks to generals, ranked marquises, and followers, each in set amounts.
translations.idiomatic: She asked the emperor to reward every general, marquis, and attendant with gold, cash, and silk.

sentences.zh: 於是董君貴寵，天下莫不聞。

translations.literal: Thereupon Lord Dong was noble and favored; none in the realm did not hear of it.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan's favor became famous throughout the empire.

sentences.zh: 郡國狗馬蹴鞠劍客輻湊董氏。

translations.literal: From commanderies and kingdoms dog handlers, horse trainers, kickball players, and sword guests converged on the Dong house.
translations.idiomatic: Adventurers, athletes, and swordsmen from every commandery flocked to his gate.

sentences.zh: 常從遊戲北宮，馳逐平樂，觀雞鞠之會，角狗馬之足，上大歡樂之。

translations.literal: He constantly followed in sport at the Northern Palace, raced at Pingle, watched cock-kickball meets, matched dogs and horses—the emperor greatly delighted in it.
translations.idiomatic: He often joined the emperor at the Northern Palace and Pingle Park for cockfights, dog races, and polo until the sovereign was wild with delight.

sentences.zh: 於是上為竇太主置酒宣室，使謁者引內董君。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor for the Grand Elder Princess Dou set wine in the Xuanshi chamber and ordered a herald to lead Lord Dong within.
translations.idiomatic: Then the emperor prepared a banquet in the Xuanshi Hall for the Elder Princess and ordered Dong Yan brought in.

sentences.zh: 是時，朔陛戟殿下，辟戟而前曰：「董偃有斬罪三，安得入乎？」

translations.literal: At that time Shuo with hall halberds below the steps, parted the halberds and stepped forward, saying: Dong Yan has three beheading crimes—how may he enter?
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo, halberd in hand below the steps, thrust forward and said, Dong Yan deserves execution three times over—how dare he enter this hall?

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何謂也？」

translations.literal: The emperor said: What does that mean?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked what he meant.

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「偃以人臣私侍公主，其罪一也。

translations.literal: Shuo said: Yan as a minister privately serves a princess—this is crime one.
translations.idiomatic: First, as a subject he has privately bedded a princess.

sentences.zh: 敗男女之化，而亂婚姻之禮，傷王制，其罪二也。」

translations.literal: He corrupts the transformation of male and female and disorders the rites of marriage, injuring royal regulation—this is crime two.
translations.idiomatic: Second, he corrupts moral teaching, violates marriage rites, and wounds the kingly institutions.

sentences.zh: 陛下富於春秋，方積思於《六經》，留神於王事，馳騖於唐、虞，折節於三代，偃不遵經勸學，反以靡麗為右，奢侈為務，盡狗馬之樂，極耳目之欲，行邪枉之道，逕淫辟之路，是乃國家之大賊，人主之大蜮。

translations.literal: Your Majesty is rich in spring and autumn, just now gathering thought on the Six Classics, fixing attention on kingly affairs, racing in Yao and Shun, bending the self to the Three Dynasties—yet Yan does not follow the classics to urge study; instead he takes dissipation as right and extravagance as business, exhausts the pleasure of dogs and horses, stretches to the limit the desires of ear and eye, walks crooked and devious paths, treads wanton and perverse roads—this is the great thief of the state, the great slug of the ruler.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty is young and should steep yourself in the Six Classics and the models of Yao, Shun, and the Three Dynasties; instead Dong Yan spurns learning, exalts luxury, fills your days with dogs, horses, and sensual delight, and leads you down depraved paths—he is a public scourge and a parasite on the throne.

sentences.zh: 偃為淫首，其罪三也。

translations.literal: Yan is the head of debauchery—this is crime three.
translations.idiomatic: Third, he is the ringleader of this vice.

sentences.zh: 昔伯姬燔而諸侯憚，奈何乎陛下？」

translations.literal: Formerly when Lady Bo Ji burned, the feudal lords were awed—what of Your Majesty?
translations.idiomatic: When Lady Bo of Song chose to die in the fire, the lords of the land trembled—will Your Majesty do less?

sentences.zh: 上默然不應良久，曰：「吾業以設飲，後而自改。」

translations.literal: The emperor was silent and did not answer for a long time, then said: I have already set the feast; afterward I will myself reform.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was silent a long while, then said, The banquet is laid; I will mend my ways hereafter.

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「不可。

translations.literal: Shuo said: It cannot.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo said, You must not.

sentences.zh: 夫宣室者，先帝之正處也，非法度之政不得入焉。

translations.literal: As for Xuanshi, it is the former emperor's correct hall; governance not of law and measure may not enter there.
translations.idiomatic: Xuanshi was the late emperor's formal hall—only lawful business may enter it.

sentences.zh: 故淫亂之漸，其變為篡，是以豎貂為淫而易牙作患，慶父死而魯國全，管、蔡誅而周室安。」

translations.literal: Therefore the gradual advance of debauchery and disorder changes into usurpation—hence Shu Diao's debauchery and Yi Ya's disaster, Qingfu's death and Lu state's preservation, Guan and Cai executed and the Zhou house secure.
translations.idiomatic: Licentiousness slides into usurpation—think of Shu Diao and Yi Ya in Qi, Qingfu in Lu, the rebels Guan and Cai in Zhou.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: Good.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, Well said.

sentences.zh: 有詔止，更置酒北宮，引董君從東司馬門。

translations.literal: An edict ordered it stopped; wine was reset at the Northern Palace; Lord Dong was led in by the east stable gate.
translations.idiomatic: He canceled the Xuanshi feast, moved the party to the Northern Palace, and had Dong Yan enter by the east stable gate.

sentences.zh: 東司馬門更名東交門。

translations.literal: The east stable gate was renamed the East Crossing Gate.
translations.idiomatic: That gate was renamed the East Crossing Gate.

sentences.zh: 賜朔黃金三十斤。

translations.literal: Shuo was granted thirty jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo received thirty jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 董君之寵由是日衰，至年三十而終。

translations.literal: Lord Dong's favor from this daily declined; he reached age thirty and died.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yan's star fell from that day; he died at thirty.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，竇太主卒，與董君會葬於霸陵。

translations.literal: Several years later the Grand Elder Princess Dou died; she was jointly buried with Lord Dong at Baling.
translations.idiomatic: Years later the Elder Princess died and was buried with Dong Yan at Baling.

sentences.zh: 是後，公主貴人多逾禮制，自董偃始。

translations.literal: After this, princesses and noble persons mostly exceeded ritual regulation—beginning from Dong Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward princesses and great ladies routinely overstepped ritual bounds—Dong Yan had shown the way.

sentences.zh: 時，天下侈靡趨末，百姓多離農畝。

translations.literal: At that time, under Heaven there was extravagant wastefulness and a rush toward secondary pursuits, and the common people for the most part left the fields of agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: In those days the realm had turned to luxury and to chasing profit over farming, and many common folk had drifted away from the plow.

sentences.zh: 上從容問朔：「吾欲化民，豈有道乎？」

translations.literal: The emperor, at his ease, asked Shuo: "I wish to transform the people — is there perhaps a Way?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked Shuo in a relaxed tone, "I want to improve the people's ways. Is there a right path to that?"

sentences.zh: 朔對曰：「堯、舜、禹、湯、文、武、成、康上古之事，經歷數千載，尚難言也，臣不敢陳。

translations.literal: Shuo replied, saying: "As for the affairs of high antiquity — Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, Wu, Cheng, and Kang — having passed through several thousand years, they are still difficult to speak of; your servant dares not set them forth.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo answered, "The deeds of Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, Wu, Cheng, and Kang lie so far back in time that even after millennia they are hard to discuss clearly; I would not presume to rehearse them here."

sentences.zh: 願近述孝文皇帝之時，當世耆老皆聞見之。

translations.literal: I wish, drawing near, to relate the time of Emperor Xiaowen, whom the venerable elders of the present age all heard of and saw.
translations.idiomatic: Instead I would speak of the reign of Emperor Xiaowen, which the old men still living today actually heard and saw for themselves.

sentences.zh: 貴為天子，富有四海，身衣弋綈，足履革舄，以韋帶劍，莞蒲為席，兵木無刃，衣縕無文，集上書囊以為殿帷；

translations.literal: Though honored as Son of Heaven and rich as to possess the four seas, on his body he wore robes of coarse black silk, on his feet he trod leather slippers; with a leather belt he suspended his sword; cattail and rushes served as his mat; weapons were of wood without sharp edges; garments of plain cloth bore no pattern; he gathered memorial document pouches to serve as hall drapes;
translations.idiomatic: Though he was exalted as Son of Heaven and possessed wealth as vast as the four seas, he dressed in plain black silk, wore simple leather shoes, girded his sword with a leather strap, sat on mats of rushes, kept wooden practice weapons without edges, wore undyed clothes without ornament, and strung together official document bags to hang as curtains in his hall;

sentences.zh: 以道德為麗，以仁義為準。

translations.literal: He took the Way and Virtue as splendor, and benevolence and righteousness as his plumb-line.
translations.idiomatic: He made moral power his finery and benevolence and righteousness his measure.

sentences.zh: 於是天下望風成俗，昭然化之。

translations.literal: Thereupon under Heaven, gazing upon the wind, customs formed, and they were openly and clearly transformed by it.
translations.idiomatic: The whole realm took its cue from him, customs took shape, and the transformation was plain for all to see.

sentences.zh: 今陛下以城中為小，圖起建章，左鳳闕，右神明，號稱千門萬戶；

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty considers the area within the walls small, and plans to raise Jianzhang — Phoenix Tower on the left, Divine Brightness on the right, proclaimed as thousand gates and myriad doors;
translations.idiomatic: Today Your Majesty finds the capital too cramped and plans to build Jianzhang Palace: Phoenix Tower to the east, the Hall of Divine Brightness to the west, advertised as a labyrinth of a thousand gates and ten thousand doors;

sentences.zh: 木土衣綺繡，狗馬被繢罽；

translations.literal: Timber and earth are clothed in figured silks; dogs and horses are draped in embroidered felts;
translations.idiomatic: Timber and plaster are swathed in brocade; hounds and horses wear patterned rugs and tapestries;

sentences.zh: 宮人簪玳瑁，垂珠璣；

translations.literal: Palace women pin tortoiseshell in their hair and hang pearl pendants;
translations.idiomatic: Palace ladies wear tortoiseshell hairpins and strings of lustrous pearls;

sentences.zh: 設戲車，教馳逐，飾文采，叢珍怪；

translations.literal: They set up trick chariots, teach racing pursuits, adorn with patterns and colors, and heap up rare curiosities;
translations.idiomatic: Trick chariots are staged, people are trained for reckless races, everything glitters with ornament, and wonders are piled everywhere;

sentences.zh: 撞萬石之鐘，擊雷霆之鼓，作俳優，舞鄭女。

translations.literal: They strike a bell of ten thousand stone capacity, beat drums like thunderclaps, put on jesters and buffoons, and have women of Zheng dance.
translations.idiomatic: Ten-thousand-stone bells are struck, drums roll like thunder, jesters caper, and dancers from Zheng whirl on the stage.

sentences.zh: 上為淫侈如此，而欲使民獨不奢侈失農，事之難者也。

translations.literal: For the sovereign to be thus licentious and extravagant, yet wish to make the people alone not extravagant and wasteful and not neglect farming — this is the difficulty of the matter.
translations.idiomatic: When the ruler indulges in such excess, expecting the common people alone to stay frugal and keep to the fields is asking for the impossible.

sentences.zh: 陛下誠能用臣朔之計，推甲乙之帳燔之於四通之衢，卻走馬示不復用，則堯、舜之隆宜可與比治矣。

translations.literal: If Your Majesty truly could employ your servant Shuo's plan — roll out the curtains of the first and second ranks and burn them at the crossroads of the four thoroughfares, send the racing horses back and show that they will not be used again — then the loftiness of Yao and Shun could fittingly be matched in good order.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty would truly adopt my plan — haul those ranked curtains into the open crossroads and burn them, retire the racehorses and show the realm they will not be raced again — then Your government might stand comparison with the golden ages of Yao and Shun.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『正其本，萬事理；

translations.literal: The Changes says: "Set straight the root, and the myriad affairs are ordered;
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says, "Straighten the root and the myriad affairs fall into line;

sentences.zh: 失之毫釐，差以千里。』

translations.literal: Miss it by a hair's breadth, and you err by a thousand li."
translations.idiomatic: miss by a hair's breadth, and you are a thousand miles astray."

sentences.zh: 願陛下留意察之。」

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to fix your mind upon this and examine it."
translations.idiomatic: I ask Your Majesty to weigh these words carefully."

sentences.zh: 朔雖詼笑，然時觀察顏色，直言切諫，上常用之。

translations.literal: Though Shuo jested and laughed, yet he constantly observed facial expressions and with straight words offered cutting remonstrance, and the emperor often employed him.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo might banter and joke, but he read the emperor's mood and spoke bluntly when remonstrance was needed; the ruler often took his advice.

sentences.zh: 自公卿在位，朔皆敖弄，無所為屈。

translations.literal: From the dukes and ministers in office on down, Shuo in every case treated them with playful irreverence and in nothing bent himself low.
translations.idiomatic: From the highest ministers on down, he teased and needled everyone in power and never humbled himself before them.

sentences.zh: 上以朔口諧辭給，好作問之。

translations.literal: Because Shuo's mouth was harmonious and his words quick, the emperor delighted in posing questions to him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor enjoyed quizzing him, for Shuo had a nimble tongue and a ready wit.

sentences.zh: 嘗問朔曰：「先生視朕何如主也？」

translations.literal: He once asked Shuo, saying: "Sir, in your view, what sort of ruler am I?"
translations.idiomatic: On one occasion he asked, "Sir, what kind of sovereign do you take me to be?"

sentences.zh: 朔對曰：「自唐、虞之隆，成、康之際，未足以諭當世。

translations.literal: Shuo replied, saying: "From the loftiness of Tang and Yu down to the era of Cheng and Kang — they are not sufficient to illustrate the present age.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo answered, "Not even the heights of Yao and Shun's age, nor the peace of King Cheng and King Kang, gives us a fair yardstick for today."

sentences.zh: 臣伏觀陛下功德，陳五帝之上，在三王之右。

translations.literal: Your servant, prostrate, has observed Your Majesty's merit and virtue: they are arrayed above the Five Thearchs and stand to the right hand of the Three Kings.
translations.idiomatic: From what I have seen, Your Majesty's merit and virtue rise above the Five Thearchs and outshine the Three Kings.

sentences.zh: 非若此而已，誠得天下賢士，公卿在位咸得其人矣。

translations.literal: It is not only thus; truly you have obtained the worthy men of the realm, and those who occupy the offices of duke and minister are all the right men for their posts.
translations.idiomatic: And it is not mere rhetoric: the realm's finest minds truly serve you, and every high office is filled by the right person.

sentences.zh: 譬若以周、邵為丞相，孔丘為御史大夫，太公為將軍，畢公高拾遺於後，弁嚴子為衛尉，皋陶為大理，後稷為司農，伊尹為少府，子贛使外國，顏、閔為博士，子夏為太常，益為右扶風，季路為執金吾，契為鴻臚，龍逢為宗正，伯夷為京兆，管仲為馮翊，魯般為將作，仲山甫為光祿，申伯為太僕，延陵季子為水衡，百里奚為典屬國，柳下惠為大長秋，史魚為司直，蘧伯玉為太傅，孔父為詹事，孫叔敖為諸侯相，子產為郡守，王慶忌為期門，夏育為鼎官，羿為旄頭，宋萬為式道侯。」

translations.literal: It is as if you had made the Duke of Zhou and the Duke of Shao your chancellors, Confucius your imperial counselor, the Grand Duke your general, the Duke of Bi, Gao, remonstrator behind the throne; Bian Yanzi as commandant of the guards; Gao Yao as grand judge of trials; Hou Ji as minister of agriculture; Yi Yin as minister of the lesser treasury; Zigong sent to foreign states; Yan and Min as erudits; Zixia as grand master of ceremonies; Yi as governor of Youfufeng; Jilu as bearer of the golden mace; Xie as grand herald; Long Feng as director of the imperial clan; Boyi as metropolitan governor; Guan Zhong as governor of Fengyi; Lu Ban as superintendent of works; Zhong Shanfu as superintendent of the imperial household; the Earl of Shen as grand coachman; Prince Jizi of Yanling as superintendent of the imperial parks; Baili Xi as director of dependent states; Liuxia Hui as grand director of the palace; Shi Yu as rectifier of conduct; Qu Boyu as grand tutor; Kong Fu as supervisor of the heir apparent; Sunshu Ao as chancellor to the feudal lords; Zichan as commandery governor; Wang Qingji as commander of the palace gates; Xia Yu as cauldron officer; Yi as vanguard chief; Song Wan as marquis who clears the road."
translations.idiomatic: It is as though the Duke of Zhou and the Duke of Shao were your chancellors, Confucius your chief censor, the Grand Duke your commander-in-chief, the Duke of Bi your remonstrating counselor at court, Bian Yanzi your captain of the guard, Gao Yao your chief judge, Hou Ji your minister of agriculture, Yi Yin your steward of the palace treasury, Zigong your envoy abroad, Yan Hui and Min Ziqian your court scholars, Zixia your master of rites, Yi your western capital intendant, Zhong You your chief of police, Xie your master of ceremonies for guests, Long Feng your keeper of the imperial genealogy, Boyi your governor of the capital region, Guan Zhong your northern intendant, Lu Ban your chief engineer, Zhong Shanfu your chamberlain for the palace, the Earl of Shen your master of the horse, Prince Jizi of Yanling your keeper of the parks and pools, Baili Xi your commissioner over dependent peoples, Liuxia Hui your chief eunuch, Shi Yu your censor of conduct, Qu Boyu your grand tutor, Kong Fu your tutor to the heir, Sunshu Ao your chief minister to the feudal lords, Zichan your prefect, Wang Qingji your palace gate captain, Xia Yu your ritual cauldron officer, Yi your spearhead guard, and Song Wan your road-clearing escort commander."

sentences.zh: 上乃大笑。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor greatly laughed.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor burst out laughing.

sentences.zh: 是時，朝廷多賢材，上復問朔：「方今公孫丞相，□大夫、董仲舒、夏侯始昌、司馬相如、吾丘壽王、主父偃、硃買臣、嚴助、汲黯、膠倉、終軍、嚴安、徐樂、司馬遷之倫，皆辯知閎達，溢於文辭，先生自視，何與比哉？」

translations.literal: At this time the court had many worthy talents, and the emperor again asked Shuo: "At present Chancellor Gongsun, the Imperial Counselor (lacuna) grandee, Dong Zhongshu, Xiahou Shichang, Sima Xiangru, Wuqiu Shouwang, Zhufu Yan, Zhu Maichen, Yan Zhu, Ji Yan, Jiao Cang, Zhong Jun, Yan An, Xu Le, Sima Qian, and their like — all are eloquent, wise, broad, and accomplished, overflowing in literary phrasing; in your own view, sir, how do you compare with them?"
translations.idiomatic: The court at that time teemed with talent, and the emperor pressed Shuo again: "Consider Chancellor Gongsun, the Imperial Counselor (name missing in text), Dong Zhongshu, Xiahou Shichang, Sima Xiangru, Wuqiu Shouwang, Zhufu Yan, Zhu Maichen, Yan Zhu, Ji Yan, Jiao Cang, Zhong Jun, Yan An, Xu Le, Sima Qian, and men of that stamp — all of them learned, articulate, and copious on the page. How do you measure yourself against such company?"

sentences.zh: 朔對曰：「臣觀其□齒牙，樹頰胲，吐脣吻，擢項頤，結股腳，連脽尻，遺蛇其跡，行步偊旅，臣朔雖不肖，尚兼此數子者。」

translations.literal: Shuo replied, saying: "Your servant has observed their (lacuna) teeth and gums, their jutting cheekbones, their thrusting lips and mouths, their stretched necks and chins, their knotted thighs and shanks, their buttocks joined to tailbones, their tracks winding like a snake's, their gait shambling and unsteady — though your servant Shuo is unworthy, I still combine in myself these several sons' traits."
translations.idiomatic: Shuo answered, "What I see in them is (lacuna) bared teeth and gums, cheeks swollen like bellows, lips forever flapping, necks craning and chins wagging, legs tangled like knotted cord, rumps welded to haunches, footprints that zigzag like a snake's trail, and a shambling, knock-kneed walk. Unworthy as I am, I flatter myself that I already embody every one of those fine qualities."

sentences.zh: 朔之進對澹辭，皆此類也。」

translations.literal: As for Shuo's advancing replies, his placid phrases — all were of this kind."
translations.idiomatic: When Shuo stepped forward to answer, his mild, understated words were always in this vein."

sentences.zh: 武帝既招英俊，程其器能，用之如不及。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu, having summoned outstanding men, measured their capacities and abilities, and employed them as if he could not catch up.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu had called the best minds to court, weighed what each could do, and put them to work as if he feared he might never hire fast enough.

sentences.zh: 時方外事胡、越，內興制度，國家多事，自公孫弘以下至司馬遷，皆奉使方外，或為郡國守相至公卿，而朔嘗至太中大夫，後常為郎，與枚皋、郭舍人俱在左右，詼啁而已。

translations.literal: At the time they were just then conducting external affairs with the Hu and Yue, and internally raising up institutions; the state had many tasks. From Gongsun Hong down to Sima Qian, all were sent on missions beyond the borders, or served as shepherds and chancellors of commanderies and kingdoms up to duke and minister rank, whereas Shuo had once reached grand counselor of the palace, but afterward was normally a gentleman-attendant; together with Mei Gao and Guo Sheren he was only at the left and right, jesting and bantering, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: While the empire was busy with the Xiongnu and the Yue on the frontier and with new institutions at home, everyone from Gongsun Hong down to Sima Qian was dispatched abroad or made a regional governor or rose to the highest offices. Shuo had briefly reached grand counselor of the palace, but he usually held only the modest rank of gentleman-attendant and, with Mei Gao and Guo Sheren, hovered at the ruler's elbow cracking jokes.

sentences.zh: 久之，朔上書陳農戰強國之計，因自訟獨不得大官，欲求試用。

translations.literal: After a long while, Shuo submitted a memorial setting forth plans for farming, warfare, and strengthening the state, and on that occasion sued himself, saying that he alone had not obtained high office, and wished to seek a trial appointment.
translations.idiomatic: In time he memorialized with schemes for agriculture, war, and national strength, lamenting that he alone had never been given a weighty post and asking to be tried in a serious role.

sentences.zh: 其言專商鞅、韓非之語也，指意放蕩，頗復詼諧，辭數萬言，終不見用。

translations.literal: His words were exclusively the language of Shang Yang and Han Fei; his intent was unrestrained, and again rather humorous; the phrasing ran to several tens of thousands of characters, yet in the end he was not employed.
translations.idiomatic: The piece was pure Shang Yang and Han Fei in tone, loose in structure and laced with humor; it ran to many thousands of characters and was never adopted.

sentences.zh: 朔因著論，設客難己，用位卑以自慰諭。

translations.literal: Thereupon Shuo composed a treatise, setting up a guest who rebuked himself, using low position to console and instruct himself.
translations.idiomatic: So he wrote a philosophical dialogue, inventing a guest who rebuked him, to console himself for his low rank.

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：

translations.literal: Its words say:
translations.idiomatic: The text begins:

sentences.zh: 客難東方朔曰：「蘇秦、張儀一當萬乘之主，而都卿相之位，澤及後世。

translations.literal: A guest rebuked Dongfang Shuo, saying: "Su Qin and Zhang Yi, each in one encounter with a ruler of ten thousand chariots, then occupied minister and chancellor positions, and their bounty reached later generations.
translations.idiomatic: The guest reproached Dongfang Shuo: "Su Qin and Zhang Yi each won an audience with a great king and walked away as chief minister; their good fortune lasted for generations.

sentences.zh: 今子大夫修先王之術，慕聖人之義，諷誦《詩》、《書》、百家之言，不可勝數，著於竹帛，脣腐齒落，服膺而不釋，好學樂道之效，明白甚矣；

translations.literal: Now you, sir, cultivate the arts of the former kings, admire the righteousness of the sages, intone and chant the Odes, Documents, and the words of the hundred schools — beyond counting — inscribe them on bamboo and silk; your lips are worn, your teeth have fallen; you clasp them to your breast and do not release them; the effect of loving learning and delighting in the Way is exceedingly clear;
translations.idiomatic: You, sir, have steeped yourself in the ways of the ancient kings and the teachings of the sages; you have memorized the Odes, the Documents, and more of the masters than anyone can count, and committed it all to writing; you have worn your lips thin and your teeth out clutching those texts to your heart. Your devotion to learning is obvious to everyone;

sentences.zh: 自以智能海內無雙，則可謂博聞辯智矣。

translations.literal: You consider your intelligence and wisdom within the seas without peer — then one may call you broadly learned and eloquently wise.
translations.idiomatic: you believe your wit unmatched under heaven — no one would deny you are learned and clever.

sentences.zh: 然悉力盡忠以事聖帝，曠日持久，官不過侍郎，位不過執戟，意者尚有遺行邪？

translations.literal: Yet, exhausting strength and exhausting loyalty to serve the sage emperor, days stretching long and time enduring, your office has not passed attendant-in-waiting, your rank has not passed halberd-bearer — is it perhaps that there are still conduct faults remaining?
translations.idiomatic: Yet though you have given the sage emperor your utmost loyalty year after year, your title has never risen above attendant-in-waiting and your duties never beyond holding the halberd at court. Are you hiding some moral flaw?

sentences.zh: 同胞之徒無所容居，其故何也？」

translations.literal: Your fellows of the same womb have nowhere to lodge — what is the reason for this?"
translations.idiomatic: Even your own kindred can barely house themselves. How do you explain that?"

sentences.zh: 東方先生喟然長息，仰而應之曰：「是固非子之所能備也。

translations.literal: Master Dongfang heaved a long sigh, looked up, and replied to him, saying: "This is indeed not what you, sir, are able to comprehend fully.
translations.idiomatic: Master Dongfang drew a long breath, lifted his eyes, and said, "That is something you simply are not in a position to understand.

sentences.zh: 彼一時也，此一時也，豈可同哉？

translations.literal: That was one season; this is one season — how could they be the same?
translations.idiomatic: Their age was one world; ours is another. How can you equate them?

sentences.zh: 夫蘇秦、張儀之時，周室大壞，諸侯不朝，力政爭權，相禽以兵，並為十二國，未有雌雄，得士者強，失士者亡，故談說行焉。

translations.literal: In the time of Su Qin and Zhang Yi, the Zhou house was greatly broken; the feudal lords did not attend court; they used forceful government and struggled for power, seized one another with arms, and stood together as twelve states; there was not yet victor or vanquished; he who obtained knights was strong, he who lost knights perished — therefore persuasion and doctrine went into practice.
