translations.idiomatic: In Su Qin and Zhang Yi's day the house of Zhou was crumbling, the lords ignored the king, might made right, and rival armies carved the realm into a dozen warring states with no clear winner. Strength went to whoever won the talent; ruin to whoever lost it. Of course wandering persuaders flourished.

sentences.zh: 身處尊位，珍寶充內，外有廩倉，澤及後世，子孫長享。

translations.literal: The body occupied honored position; treasures filled the interior; outside there were granaries and storehouses; the bounty reached later generations, and sons and grandsons enjoyed it long.
translations.idiomatic: A successful strategist sat in high office, vaults groaning with treasure, granaries overflowing, and left a legacy his descendants lived on for generations.

sentences.zh: 今則不然。

translations.literal: Now it is not so.
translations.idiomatic: Today nothing of the sort applies.

sentences.zh: 聖帝流德，天下震懾，諸侯賓服，連四海之外以為帶，安於覆盂，動猶運之掌，賢不肖何以異哉？

translations.literal: The sage emperor spreads virtue; under Heaven trembles in awe; the feudal lords guest-submit; he links what lies beyond the four seas to serve as his belt; he is secure as an inverted bowl; movement is like turning it in the palm — how should worthy and unworthy differ?
translations.idiomatic: Our sage emperor's virtue overawes the realm; the lords come as guests; the four seas beyond the borders hang from his belt like a sash; the empire sits as steady as an upturned bowl; he moves it as easily as spinning something in his palm. In such a world, what separates the worthy from the worthless?

sentences.zh: 遵天之道，順地之理，物無不得其所；

translations.literal: Following Heaven's Way, complying with Earth's pattern — among things none fails to obtain its proper place;
translations.idiomatic: Heaven has its constant course, earth its proper order, and every creature finds its allotted place;

sentences.zh: 故綏之則安，動之則苦；

translations.literal: therefore, soothe them and they are at peace; stir them and they suffer;
translations.idiomatic: soothe the people and they rest; harry them and they groan;

sentences.zh: 尊之則為將，卑之則為虜；

translations.literal: honor them and they become generals; humble them and they become captives;
translations.idiomatic: honor a man and he becomes a general; break him and he is a prisoner;

sentences.zh: 抗之則在青雲之上，抑之則在深泉之下；

translations.literal: raise them and they are above the blue clouds; suppress them and they are below the deep springs;
translations.idiomatic: lift him and he rides the clouds; cast him down and he sinks into the abyss;

sentences.zh: 用之則為虎，不用則為鼠；

translations.literal: employ them and they are tigers; do not employ them and they are rats;
translations.idiomatic: use him and he is a tiger; ignore him and he is a mouse;

sentences.zh: 雖欲盡節效情，安知前後？

translations.literal: Though one wishes to exhaust integrity and offer one's utmost feeling, how does one know what comes before and after?
translations.idiomatic: even if you mean to give your whole heart to the throne, who can tell whether you will rise or fall?

sentences.zh: 夫天地之大，士民之眾，竭精談說，並進輻湊者不可勝數，悉力募之，困於衣食，或失門戶。

translations.literal: As for the greatness of Heaven and Earth and the multitude of knights and commoners — those who exhaust their essence in talk and doctrine, who advance together like spokes to the hub, cannot be counted; exhaust strength to recruit them, yet they are trapped in clothing and food, or lose their gate and door.
translations.idiomatic: The world is vast, the educated class huge; countless men crowd the capital peddling advice like spokes to a hub. The ruler can summon them all, yet many still starve for want of a meal or never find a patron.

sentences.zh: 使蘇秦、張儀與僕並生於今之世，曾不得掌故，安敢望常侍郎乎？

translations.literal: Suppose Su Qin and Zhang Yi were born together with your servant in the present age — they would not even obtain the post of archivist; how would they dare to hope for regular attendant-in-waiting?
translations.idiomatic: If Su Qin and Zhang Yi were reborn today, they would be lucky to land a clerk's job, let alone a regular attendant-in-waiting like me.

sentences.zh: 故曰時異事異。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: the times differ, the affairs differ.
translations.idiomatic: So the saying goes: different times, different rules."

sentences.zh: 「雖然，安可以不務修身乎哉！

translations.literal: "Even so, how can one fail to devote oneself to cultivating the person!
translations.idiomatic: "Even so," he went on, "can anyone afford to neglect self-cultivation?

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『鼓鐘於宮，聲聞於外。』

translations.literal: The Odes says: "The drums and bells in the palace, the sound is heard outside."
translations.idiomatic: The Odes says, "Beat the drum and ring the bell inside the hall, and the sound carries beyond the walls."

sentences.zh: 『鶴鳴於九皋，聲聞於天。』

translations.literal: "The crane cries in the nine marshes, the sound is heard in Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: "The crane cries from the distant marsh, yet Heaven hears it."

sentences.zh: 苟能修身，何患不榮！

translations.literal: If indeed one can cultivate the person, what worry is there of not being honored!
translations.idiomatic: Cultivate yourself earnestly, and honor will follow — why doubt it?

sentences.zh: 太公體行仁義，七十有二乃設用於文、武，得信厥說，封於齊，七百歲而不絕。

translations.literal: The Grand Duke embodied and practiced benevolence and righteousness; only at seventy-two did he establish employment with Wen and Wu; he obtained trust for his doctrines, was enfeoffed in Qi, and for seven hundred years did not perish.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Duke lived out benevolence and righteousness; not until he was seventy-two did Kings Wen and Wu put his counsel to use. They believed him, enfeoffed him in Qi, and his line lasted seven hundred years.

sentences.zh: 此士所以日夜孳孳，敏行而不敢怠也。

translations.literal: This is why knights day and night are diligent, act keenly, and dare not slacken.
translations.idiomatic: That is why a gentleman toils day and night and never dares slacken his step.

sentences.zh: 辟若鶺鴒，飛且鳴矣。

translations.literal: It is like the wagtail — flying and also crying.
translations.idiomatic: Think of the wagtail on the wing, calling as it flies."

sentences.zh: 傳曰：『天不為人之惡寒而輟其冬，地不為人之惡險而輟其廣，君子不為小人之匈匈而易其行。』

translations.literal: The tradition says: "Heaven does not, because humans detest cold, cease its winter; Earth does not, because humans detest precipitousness, cease its breadth; the gentleman does not, because petty men clamor, change his conduct."
translations.idiomatic: The tradition runs, "Heaven does not cancel winter because men hate the cold; earth does not shrink its breadth because men fear the heights; the gentleman does not change his course because petty people shout him down."

sentences.zh: 『天有常度，地有常形，君子有常行；

translations.literal: "Heaven has constant measure, Earth has constant form, the gentleman has constant conduct;
translations.idiomatic: "Heaven keeps its seasons, earth its contours, the gentleman his steady practice;

sentences.zh: 君子道其常，小人計其功。』

translations.literal: the gentleman follows his constant; the petty man reckons his merit."
translations.idiomatic: the gentleman cleaves to what endures; the small man chases every advantage."

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『禮義之不愆，何恤人之言？』

translations.literal: The Odes says: "If in ritual and righteousness one does not transgress, what anxiety about others' words?"
translations.idiomatic: The Odes adds, "If you never stray from ritual and right, why fear gossip?"

sentences.zh: 故曰：『水至清則無魚，人至察則無徒。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: "When water is too clear there are no fish; when a man is too scrutinizing there are no companions.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the proverb: fish cannot live in water that is too clear; a man who picks at every fault will have no friends.

sentences.zh: 冕而前旒，所以蔽明；

translations.literal: The cap with pendants before the eyes — by this means to screen brightness;
translations.idiomatic: The king's crown hangs beaded curtains before his eyes to dim the glare;

sentences.zh: 黈纊充耳，所以塞聰。』

translations.literal: Yellow cotton fills the ears — by this means to block keen hearing."
translations.idiomatic: Yellow silk plugs his ears to muffle sharp sounds."

sentences.zh: 明有所不見，聰有所不聞，舉大德，赦小過，無求備於一人之義也。

translations.literal: Brightness has what it does not see; keen hearing has what it does not hear; raising great virtue, pardoning small faults — this is the meaning of not seeking completeness in a single person.
translations.idiomatic: Even clear sight misses something; even sharp hearing fails somewhere. Reward great virtue, overlook petty slips — that is what it means not to demand perfection of any one man.

sentences.zh: 枉而直之，使自得之；

translations.literal: Bend them and straighten them, causing them to obtain it themselves;
translations.idiomatic: Set crooked timber straight, but let the tree find its own grain;

sentences.zh: 優而柔之，使自求之；

translations.literal: Indulge them and soften them, causing them to seek it themselves;
translations.idiomatic: Coax and soften it, but let it seek its own shape;

sentences.zh: 揆而度之，使自索之。

translations.literal: Measure and estimate them, causing them to search it out themselves.
translations.idiomatic: measure and weigh it, but let the truth declare itself.

sentences.zh: 蓋聖人教化如此，欲自得之；

translations.literal: Generally the sage's transforming instruction is thus — he desires that they obtain it themselves;
translations.idiomatic: The sage teaches in just this way because he wants people to discover the lesson for themselves;

sentences.zh: 自得之，則敏且廣矣。

translations.literal: when they obtain it themselves, then they are keen and broad.
translations.idiomatic: and what they learn for themselves is quick to take root and broad in reach."

sentences.zh: 「今世之處士，魁然無徒，廓然獨居，上觀許由，下察接輿，計同范蠡，忠合子胥，天下和平，與義相扶，寡耦少徒，固其宜也，子何疑於我哉？

translations.literal: "Now the recluses of the age — towering, without followers; vastly alone in dwelling; above they gaze on Xu You, below they examine Jie Yu; in planning they match Fan Li, in loyalty they accord with Zixu; under Heaven is peace and harmony, they support one another with righteousness — few mates, few followers — solidly it is fitting; why do you, sir, doubt me?
translations.idiomatic: "Today's hermits stand aloof like giants without a retinue, scan the past for Xu You and Jie Yu, compare their schemes to Fan Li and their loyalty to Wu Zixu, yet live in a tranquil age where righteousness needs no loud champion. Of course they walk alone with few companions. Why should you find that strange in me?

sentences.zh: 若夫燕之用樂毅，秦之任李斯，酈食其之下齊，說行如流，曲從如環，所欲必得，功若丘山，海內定，國家安，是遇其時也，子又何怪之邪？

translations.literal: As for Yan's employing Yue Yi, Qin's appointing Li Si, Li Yiji's subduing of Qi — persuasion went like flowing water, bending compliance like a ring; what they desired they invariably obtained; merit was like hills and mountains; within the seas there was stability, the state and family were secure — this was encountering their season; why, sir, do you again find it strange?
translations.idiomatic: When Yan used Yue Yi, when Qin trusted Li Si, when Li Yiji talked Qi into surrender, advice flowed like water and rulers bent like rings on a chain; every wish was granted, achievements piled up like mountains, and the realm knew peace. Those men simply met their moment. What is odd about that?

sentences.zh: 語曰『以管窺天，以蠡測海，以莛撞鐘』，豈能通其條貫，考其文理，發其音聲哉！

translations.literal: A saying states, "With a tube to peep at Heaven, with a gourd-shell to measure the sea, with a reed to strike a bell" — how could one penetrate its systematic threads, examine its literary pattern, or bring forth its sounds!
translations.idiomatic: There is an old line about judging the sky through a bamboo tube, the ocean with a ladling cup, or a bell with a beanstalk — how could you hope to trace its structure, read its grain, or hear its true voice?

sentences.zh: 繇是觀之，譬猶鼱鼩之襲狗，孤豚之咋虎，至則靡耳，何功之有？

translations.literal: From this observing it, it is like shrew-mice attacking a dog, a lone piglet biting a tiger — when they arrive they are pulverized — what merit is there?
translations.idiomatic: By that measure, you might as well send a shrew to ambush a hound or a lone piglet to nip a tiger: the moment they meet, they are pulp. What feat is that?

sentences.zh: 今以下愚而非處士，雖欲勿困，固不得已，此適足以明其不知權變而終或於大道也。」

translations.literal: Now, with inferior foolishness to condemn the recluse, though one wishes not to be trapped, solidly there is no choice; this just suffices to show that they do not know expedient adaptation and in the end are bewildered concerning the great Way."
translations.idiomatic: To assail a recluse with such bottom-tier folly is to invite entanglement you cannot escape; it only proves you grasp neither circumstance nor timing and end utterly bewildered concerning the great Way."

sentences.zh: 又設非有先生之論，其辭曰：

translations.literal: Again he set up the disquisition of Mr. Feiyou; its words say:
translations.idiomatic: He also composed the dialogue of "Mr. Never-Was," which begins:

sentences.zh: 非有先生仕於吳，進不稱往古以厲主意，退不能揚君美以顯其功，默然無言者三年矣。

translations.literal: Mr. Feiyou served in Wu; advancing he did not cite antiquity to sharpen the ruler's intent; retreating he could not spread the lord's excellence to display his merit; silently without words — three years already.
translations.idiomatic: Mr. Never-Was had taken office in Wu. For three years he had not advanced a single precedent to sharpen the king's judgment, nor stepped back to praise his lord and burnish his own service. He had simply held his tongue.

sentences.zh: 吳王怪而問之，曰：「寡人獲先人之功，寄於眾賢之上，夙興夜寐，未嘗敢怠也。

translations.literal: The king of Wu wondered and questioned him, saying: "I have obtained my forebears' merit and rest above the multitude of worthies; I rise early and sleep late; I have never dared to slacken.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Wu, puzzled, demanded an explanation: "I inherited my ancestors' achievement and rule over a host of worthy men. I rise before dawn and retire late; I have never dared slacken.

sentences.zh: 今先生率然高舉，遠集吳地，將以輔治寡人，誠竊嘉之，體不安席，食不甘味，目不視靡曼之色，耳不聽鐘鼓之音，虛心定志欲聞流議者三年於茲矣。

translations.literal: Now you, sir, suddenly raised yourself high and gathered from afar in the land of Wu; you will thereby assist in governing me — I truly privately rejoice in it; my body is not at ease on the mat, my food is not sweet to the taste; my eyes do not gaze on luxurious beauty; my ears do not listen to bell and drum sounds; with empty heart and settled intent I have wished to hear flowing discourse — three years up to now.
translations.idiomatic: You, sir, came from afar to settle in Wu and promised to help me govern; I welcomed you with all my heart. For three years I have tossed on my mat, found my food tasteless, shut my eyes to silks and dancers, silenced music in my halls, and steadied my mind, longing to hear whatever counsel you would pour forth.

sentences.zh: 今先生進無以輔治，退不揚主譽，竊不為先生取之也。

translations.literal: Now you, sir, advancing have nothing wherewith to assist governance, retreating do not raise the lord's reputation — I privately do not take this for you, sir.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you bring no counsel forward and no praise backward. I cannot approve of that, sir.

sentences.zh: 蓋懷能而不見，是不忠也；

translations.literal: Generally, cherishing ability yet not displaying it — this is disloyalty;
translations.idiomatic: To hoard talent and hide it is disloyalty;

sentences.zh: 見而不行，主不明也。

translations.literal: displaying it yet not acting — this is the lord's lack of clarity.
translations.idiomatic: to display it and see no response marks an obtuse ruler.

sentences.zh: 意者寡人殆不明乎？」

translations.literal: Perhaps I am perhaps unclear?"
translations.idiomatic: Tell me plainly — is the fault mine?"

sentences.zh: 非有先生伏而唯唯。

translations.literal: Mr. Feiyou prostrated himself and assented with "yes-yes."
translations.idiomatic: Mr. Never-Was bowed low and muttered assent.

sentences.zh: 吳王曰：「可以談矣，寡人將竦意而覽焉。」

translations.literal: The king of Wu said: "It is permissible to speak; I will prick up my intent and peruse it."
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "You may speak. I am listening with full attention."

sentences.zh: 先生曰：「於戲！

translations.literal: The gentleman said: "Alas!
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman cried, "Alas!

sentences.zh: 可乎哉？

translations.literal: Can it be?
translations.idiomatic: Can it be done?

sentences.zh: 可乎哉？

translations.literal: Can it be?
translations.idiomatic: Can it truly be done?

sentences.zh: 談何容易！

translations.literal: To speak — how is it easy!
translations.idiomatic: Easy talk is the hardest thing in the world!

sentences.zh: 夫談有悖於目、拂於耳、謬於心而便於身者；

translations.literal: Now, discourse has that which is contrary to the eyes, brushes against the ears, errs in the heart, yet benefits the person;
translations.idiomatic: Some counsel offends the eye, grates on the ear, and wounds pride even as it saves the listener;

sentences.zh: 或有說於目、順於耳、快於心而毀於行者。

translations.literal: or there is that which pleases the eyes, complies with the ears, delights the heart, yet ruins conduct.
translations.idiomatic: other words delight the senses and flatter the mind yet ruin a man in the doing.

sentences.zh: 非有明王聖主，孰能聽之？」

translations.literal: Unless there is a perspicacious king or sage lord, who can listen to it?"
translations.idiomatic: Without an enlightened sovereign, who could bear to hear the first sort?"

sentences.zh: 吳王曰：「何為其然也？

translations.literal: The king of Wu said: "Why is it thus?
translations.idiomatic: The king asked, "Why should that be?

sentences.zh: 『中人已上可以語上也。』

translations.literal: "Those of middling man and above may be spoken to about what is high."
translations.idiomatic: They say anyone above middling intelligence can discuss the highest matters."

sentences.zh: 先生試言，寡人將聽焉。」

translations.literal: Sir, try to speak; I will listen."
translations.idiomatic: Speak, sir. I am ready to listen."

sentences.zh: 先生對曰：「昔者關龍逢深諫於桀，而王子比干直言於紂，此二臣者，皆極慮盡忠，閔王澤不下流，而萬民騷動，故直言其失，切諫其邪者，將以為君之榮，除主之禍也。

translations.literal: The gentleman replied, saying: Formerly Guan Longpeng remonstrated deeply with Jie, and Prince Bigan spoke straight with Zhou—these two ministers both exhausted thought and fulfilled loyalty, pitied that the king's grace did not flow downward while the myriad people were restless; therefore they spoke straight of his faults and cut remonstrance of his wickedness, intending thereby to be the lord's glory and remove the ruler's disaster.
translations.idiomatic: Mr. Feiyou answered: Long ago Guan Longpeng remonstrated fearlessly with Jie and Bigan spoke blunt truth to Zhou. Both gave their utmost loyalty, grieving that royal kindness never reached the people while the masses writhed in disorder. They named the ruler's faults and attacked his vices in order to bring him honor and spare him calamity.

sentences.zh: 今則不然，反以為誹謗君之行，無人臣之禮，果紛然傷於身，蒙不辜之名，戮及先人，為天下笑，故曰談何容易！

translations.literal: Now it is not so; instead they are taken as slandering the lord's conduct, lacking the rite of minister to lord; in the end tumultuously they are harmed in the body, bear undeserved names, execution reaches their forefathers, and they become the laughter of the realm—therefore it is said, how easy to speak!
translations.idiomatic: Today the opposite happens: straight speech is called slander and treated as breach of fealty. The speaker is broken on the wheel, smeared with false guilt, his ancestors disgraced, and the world mocks his corpse—so the proverb says, Easier said than done.

sentences.zh: 是以輔弼之臣瓦解，而邪諂之人並進，遂及蜚廉、惡來革等，二人皆詐偽，巧言利口以進其身，陰奉雕□刻鏤之好以納其心。

translations.literal: Therefore assisting ministers collapsed like tiles, while wicked flatterers advanced together; it reached Feilian, E Lai, and the like—the two men were both deceitful and false, clever words and sharp mouths to advance their persons, secretly presenting carved and engraved (lacuna) delights to win his heart.
translations.idiomatic: Pillars of state shattered while sycophants swarmed the court—men like Feilian and E Lai, glib frauds who clawed upward with slick tongues and smuggled in every kind of carved luxury to poison the king's mind.

sentences.zh: 務快耳目之欲，以苟容為度。

translations.literal: They strove to gratify ear and eye's desires, taking mere accommodation as the measure.
translations.idiomatic: They chased whatever pleased eye and ear and called groveling good enough for government.

sentences.zh: 遂往不戒，身沒被戮，宗廟崩弛，國家為虛，放戮聖賢，親近讒夫。

translations.literal: They went forward without caution; bodies perished under execution; ancestral temples collapsed and loosened; the state became void; sages worthies were banished and slain; slanderers were drawn near.
translations.idiomatic: No warning checked their course: they died under the axe, the altars fell, the kingdom hollowed out, good men were driven off or killed, and whispering villains took their seats.

sentences.zh: 《詩》不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the Odes say?
translations.idiomatic: Did not the Odes say it?

sentences.zh: 『讒人罔極，交亂四國』，此之謂也。

translations.literal: 'Slanderers have no limit, together they disorder the four states'—this is what it speaks of.
translations.idiomatic: Slanderers know no limit; they set the four quarters ablaze—that is the verse I mean.

sentences.zh: 故卑身賤體，說色微辭，愉愉呴呴，終無益於主上之治，則志士仁人不忍為也。

translations.literal: Therefore to humble body and debase the person, pleasing countenance and slighting words, ingratiating and wheedling, in the end brings no benefit to the superior's governance—then resolute knights and humane men cannot bear to do it.
translations.idiomatic: To cringe, flatter, and simper may win a smile but never mends a realm—no man of true resolve will stoop to that.

sentences.zh: 將儼然作矜嚴之色，深言直諫，上以拂主之邪，下以損百姓之害，則忤於邪主之心，歷於衰世之法。

translations.literal: If instead one solemnly assumes stern and severe mien, speaks deeply and remonstrates straight, above to brush away the lord's evil, below to lessen the hundred surnames' harm—then one offends the wicked lord's heart and encounters the decadent age's laws.
translations.idiomatic: Yet don a stern face, speak hard truth to scrape evil from the throne and ease the people's hurt, and you will prick a vicious ruler's pride and collide with the law of a dying age.

sentences.zh: 故養壽命之士莫肯進也，遂居深山之間，積土為室，編蓬為戶，彈琴其中，以詠先王之風，亦可以樂而忘死矣。

translations.literal: Therefore gentlemen who nurture life and fate none are willing to advance; they then dwell among deep mountains, pile earth for a room, weave thicket for a door, pluck a zither within, thereby chanting the wind of former kings—thus they may rejoice and forget death.
translations.idiomatic: So men who prize their lives refuse office altogether. They heap a hut in the hills, weave a gate of brush, strum a zither, and hymn the old kings until joy makes them forget mortality.

sentences.zh: 是以伯夷、叔齊避周，餓於首陽之下，後世稱其仁。

translations.literal: Therefore Boyi and Shuqi avoided Zhou and starved below Shouyang; posterity praises their benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: Boyi and Shuqi fled the Zhou and starved on Shouyang; later ages honor their humanity.

sentences.zh: 如是，邪主之行固足畏也，故曰談何容易！」

translations.literal: If it is thus, the conduct of a wicked lord is indeed enough to fear—therefore it is said, how easy to speak!
translations.idiomatic: A depraved sovereign is terror enough—again, easier said than done.

sentences.zh: 於是吳王懼然易容，捐薦去幾，危坐而聽。

translations.literal: Thereupon the king of Wu was fearful and changed countenance; he cast aside cushion and removed the low table; sat upright and listened.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Wu blanched, pushed away his cushion and side table, straightened his back, and listened.

sentences.zh: 先生曰：「接輿避世，箕子被髮陽狂，此二人者，皆避濁世以全其身者也。

translations.literal: The gentleman said: Jieyu avoided the age; Jizi let his hair down and feigned madness—these two men both avoided a turbid age to preserve their persons.
translations.idiomatic: The master said, Jieyu fled the world and Jizi matted his hair and played the madman—both escaped a foul age to save their skins.

sentences.zh: 使遇明王聖主，得清燕之閒，寬和之色，發憤畢誠，圖畫安危，揆度得失，上以安主體，下以便萬民，則五帝、三王之道可幾而見也。

translations.literal: Had they met a bright king and sage master, obtained clear repose's leisure and mild harmonious mien, vented indignation and exhausted sincerity, plotted safety and danger, measured gain and loss, above to settle the ruler's person, below to benefit the myriad people—then the way of the Five Emperors and Three Kings could nearly be seen.
translations.idiomatic: Had they found an enlightened sovereign, leisure, and an open face, they could have poured out loyal counsel, weighed every peril and profit, steadied the throne above and eased the people below—the way of the Five Emperors and Three Kings would almost have stood within reach.

sentences.zh: 故伊尹蒙恥辱、負鼎俎、和五味以干湯，太公釣於渭之陽以見文王。

translations.literal: Therefore Yi Yin bore shame and dishonor, carried cauldron and stand, blended five flavors to seek Tang; the Grand Duke fished on the Wei's north bank (the sunny bank) to see King Wen.
translations.idiomatic: That is why Yi Yin swallowed humiliation, shouldered the kitchen cauldron, and seasoned five flavors to win Tang's ear, and the Grand Duke angled on the north bank of the Wei until King Wen found him.

sentences.zh: 心合意同，謀無不成，計無不從，誠得其君也。

translations.literal: Heart matched intent the same; no plan did not succeed, no scheme was not followed—truly they obtained their lords.
translations.idiomatic: When heart matches heart, every plan succeeds and every counsel is heeded—they had found the right lord.

sentences.zh: 深念遠慮，引義以正其身，推恩以廣其下，本仁祖義，褒有德，祿賢能，誅惡亂，總遠方，一統類，美風俗，此帝王所由昌也。

translations.literal: Deep thought and far planning, drawing rightness to rectify the self, extending grace to broaden those below, rooting in benevolence and taking rightness as ancestor, praising the virtuous, employing the worthy and able, punishing evil and disorder, gathering the distant regions, unifying kinds, beautifying customs—these are whereby emperors and kings flourish.
translations.idiomatic: Deep counsel, moral self-rule, spreading kindness below, honoring humanity and duty, rewarding the good, paying the worthy, crushing the wicked, binding far lands in one fabric, and polishing custom—this is how true kings grow great.

sentences.zh: 上不變天性，下不奪人倫，則天地和洽，遠方懷之，故號聖王。

translations.literal: Above not altering heavenly nature, below not seizing human relations—then Heaven and Earth are harmonious and concordant, distant regions cherish them; therefore they are titled sage kings.
translations.idiomatic: They did not twist Heaven's pattern nor break human ties; Heaven and Earth chimed in tune and distant peoples came willingly—hence they are called sage-kings.

sentences.zh: 臣子之職既加矣，於是裂地定封，爵為公侯，傳國子孫，名顯後世，民到於今稱之，以遇湯與文王也。

translations.literal: When the duties of minister and son were already fulfilled, then they split land and fixed fiefs, were ennobled duke or marquis, transmitted the state to sons and grandsons, names shone in later ages; the people down to today praise them, because they encountered Tang and King Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Once their service was complete, they received fiefs, noble rank, and lines that still shine in history—the people praise them yet because they met Tang and King Wen.

sentences.zh: 太公、伊尹以如此，龍逢、比干獨如彼，豈不哀哉！

translations.literal: The Grand Duke and Yi Yin were thus; Longpeng and Bigan alone were like that—is it not pitiable!
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Duke and Yi Yin won such ends; Longpeng and Bigan the other—is that not tragedy?

sentences.zh: 故曰談何容易！」

translations.literal: Therefore it is said, how easy to speak!
translations.idiomatic: So again: easier said than done.

sentences.zh: 於是吳王穆然，俯而深惟，仰而泣下交頤，曰：「嗟乎！

translations.literal: Thereupon the king of Wu was solemn; he bent and thought deeply, looked up and wept so tears crossed his cheeks, saying: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: The king of Wu sat silent, brooding, then lifted a face wet with tears and cried, Alas!

sentences.zh: 余國之不亡也，綿綿連連，殆哉，世之不絕也！」

translations.literal: That my state has not perished—continuous and unbroken—perilous indeed! That generations have not been cut off!
translations.idiomatic: That Wu still stands, generation after fragile generation, is peril itself—that our line is not yet extinct!

sentences.zh: 於是正明堂之朝，齊君臣之位，舉賢材，布德惠，施仁義，賞有功；

translations.literal: Thereupon he rectified the Bright Hall court, aligned lord and ministers' positions, raised worthy talent, spread virtuous kindness, applied benevolence and rightness, rewarded those with merit;
translations.idiomatic: He then set the Bright Hall court in order, ranked high and low, promoted talent, spread kindness, practiced humanity and duty, and rewarded merit;

sentences.zh: 躬節儉，減後宮之費，損車馬之用；

translations.literal: personally practiced frugality, reduced the rear palace's expenses, lessened chariot and horse use;
translations.idiomatic: he lived plainly, cut harem expense, and thinned the royal stables;

sentences.zh: 放鄭聲，遠佞人，省庖廚，去侈靡；

translations.literal: released Zheng music, kept flatterers at a distance, lessened kitchens, removed extravagance;
translations.idiomatic: he banned the lewd airs of Zheng, drove sycophants off, shrank the kitchens, and shed excess;

sentences.zh: 卑宮館，壞苑囿，填池塹，以予貧民無產業者；

translations.literal: humbled palaces and lodges, destroyed parks and preserves, filled pools and moats, to give to poor commoners without property;
translations.idiomatic: he lowered palace roofs, tore down hunting parks, filled ponds and ditches, and gave the land to the landless poor;

sentences.zh: 開內藏，振貧窮，存耆老，恤孤獨；

translations.literal: opened inner treasuries, relieved the poor and destitute, preserved the old, pitied orphans and solitaries;
translations.idiomatic: he opened the vaults to aid the destitute, sheltered the aged, and cared for widows and orphans;

sentences.zh: 薄賦斂，省刑辟。

translations.literal: lightened taxes and levies, reduced punishments and executions.
translations.idiomatic: he lightened taxes and eased the penal code.

sentences.zh: 行此三年，海內晏然，天下大洽，陰陽和調，萬物咸得其宜；

translations.literal: Practicing this three years, within the seas there was calm; the realm was greatly harmonized; yin and yang were tuned; the myriad things all obtained their fitness;
translations.idiomatic: Three years of such rule brought quiet to the seas, harmony to the realm, balanced yin and yang, and every creature its proper place;

sentences.zh: 國無災害之變，民無饑寒之色，家給人民，畜積有餘，囹圄空虛；

translations.literal: the state had no disaster prodigies; the people had no hunger or cold complexion; households supplied the people; stores piled surplus; prisons stood empty;
translations.idiomatic: no calamity struck the state, no face showed famine, every home had enough and granaries overflowed, and the jails stood empty;

sentences.zh: 鳳凰來集，麒麟在郊，甘露既降，硃草萌牙；

translations.literal: phoenixes came to gather, unicorns were in the suburbs, sweet dew already fell, red grass sprouted teeth;
translations.idiomatic: phoenixes alighted, unicorns roamed the suburbs, sweet dew fell, and crimson auspicious grass sprouted;

sentences.zh: 遠方異俗之人鄉風慕義，各奉其職而來朝賀。

translations.literal: People of distant regions and strange customs turned to the wind and admired rightness, each bearing his duty and coming to court in congratulation.
translations.idiomatic: barbarians from afar caught the breeze of his virtue, took office, and came to offer tribute.

sentences.zh: 故治亂之道，存亡之端，若此易見，而君人者莫肯為也，臣愚竊以為過。

translations.literal: Therefore the way of order and chaos, the tip of survival and perishing, is thus easy to see, yet those who rule men none are willing to do it—your servant foolishly privately thinks it a fault.
translations.idiomatic: The line between order and ruin is plain as this, yet no ruler will walk it—your servant counts that a grave mistake.

sentences.zh: 故《詩》云：「王國克生，惟周之楨，濟濟多士，文王以寧。」

translations.literal: Therefore the Odes say: 'In the king's domain they are born; they are Zhou's pillars; many officers in array—King Wen thereby was tranquil.'
translations.idiomatic: The Odes say, In the royal domain they are born—the timbers of Zhou; rank on rank of officers—that was how King Wen kept the peace.

sentences.zh: 此之謂也。

translations.literal: This is what it speaks of.
translations.idiomatic: That is the sense of it.

sentences.zh: 朔之文辭，此二篇最善。

translations.literal: Among Shuo's literary compositions, these two pieces are the best.
translations.idiomatic: Of Dongfang Shuo's writings, these two essays are the finest.

sentences.zh: 其餘《封泰山》、《責和氏璧》及《皇太子生禖》、《屏風》、《殿上柏柱》、《平樂觀賦獵》，八言、七言上下，《從公孫弘借車》，凡劉向所錄朔書具是矣。

translations.literal: The rest—'Feng on Mount Tai,' 'Rebuking the He Clan Jade,' and 'Prayer for the Heir Apparent's Birth,' 'Screen,' 'Cypress Pillars in the Hall,' 'Rhapsody on the Hunt at Pingle Lodge,' eight-character and seven-character lines upper and lower, 'Borrowing a Carriage from Gongsun Hong'—all that Liu Xiang recorded of Shuo's writings is here.
translations.idiomatic: The remainder—his pieces on the Feng sacrifice at Mount Tai, the rebuke to the He-family jade, the birth prayer for the crown prince, the screen, the hall cypress pillars, the Pingle hunt rhapsody, suites of eight- and seven-syllable lines, and the skit on borrowing a carriage from Gongsun Hong—comprise everything Liu Xiang catalogued under Dongfang Shuo's name.

sentences.zh: 世所傳他事皆非也。

translations.literal: Other matters transmitted by the world are all not so.
translations.idiomatic: The other anecdotes told of him in later times are untrue.
